name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry point to search, browse catalog categories, review featured products, and access the custom tarp builder, cart, and favorites for industrial tarps and protective covers.

core_features: Global product search input that lets users enter a query and navigate to the catalog page with the query applied, with optional inline search suggestions based on the searchProducts interface.
Main navigation categories in the header populated from getMainNavigationCategories, providing direct links into catalog category views (e.g., tarps by material, tarps by feature, protective covers).
Homepage quick-access category entry points (tiles or list) that deep-link users into key catalog categories using category IDs returned from getMainNavigationCategories.
Featured products section populated from getHomepageFeaturedProducts, allowing users to click through to individual product detail pages.
Header cart summary using getCartSummary to show the current cart quantity and subtotal, with navigation to the full cart page.
Header access points to the custom tarp builder and favorites, navigating to their respective pages.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Search for products from Home
1. User focuses the global search input.
2. User types a search phrase.
3. As the user types, the component optionally calls searchProducts with the current query and displays non-blocking inline suggestions (names and/or key attributes of matching Products).
4. User reviews suggestions; optionally clicks a suggestion:
   - onSuggestionClick navigates to product.html with ?id=<selectedProduct.id>.
5. Alternatively, user submits the search form (e.g., presses Enter or clicks the search button).
6. onSubmit constructs a URL catalog.html?query=<encodedSearchTerm> and navigates to it.
7. The catalog page then uses the query parameter to perform the actual result listing (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: Enter catalog via main navigation categories in the header
1. On page load, the header component calls getMainNavigationCategories.
2. The component renders one navigation link per Category returned.
3. User clicks a category navigation link.
4. onCategoryClick reads the associated Category.id and navigates to catalog.html?categoryId=<Category.id>.
5. The catalog page uses the categoryId parameter to filter products (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: Use homepage quick-access category entry points
1. On page load, the homepage category entry component consumes Category data obtained from getMainNavigationCategories (either directly or via the header component).
2. The component filters categories to those whose IDs correspond to key catalog groups (e.g., tarps_by_material, tarps_by_feature, protective_covers), using only the IDs actually present in the response.
3. For each such Category, the component renders a clickable entry point.
4. User clicks an entry point.
5. onCategoryTileClick navigates to catalog.html?categoryId=<Category.id> based on the clicked item.

Workflow: Explore featured products from Home
1. On page load, the featured products component calls getHomepageFeaturedProducts.
2. The component renders a list/grid of Product items using the returned data.
3. User scans product names, key attributes, and prices as needed.
4. User clicks a featured product card or its primary action.
5. onProductClick navigates to product.html?id=<Product.id>.
6. Product details are loaded on the product page (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: View cart summary and go to cart
1. On page load, the header cart component calls getCartSummary.
2. The component binds cart.total_quantity and subtotal or estimated_total_before_shipping_and_tax to the cart indicator (e.g., item count and mini total).
3. User clicks the cart indicator in the header.
4. onCartClick navigates to cart.html (URL details governed by the cart page).
5. Optionally, hovering or clicking can toggle a mini-cart dropdown showing basic information about items, using the items array from getCartSummary (no modifications are made here).

Workflow: Access custom tarp builder from Home
1. User locates the "Build Your Custom Tarp" access point in the header or a clearly labeled call-to-action area on the Home page.
2. User clicks the access point.
3. onCustomBuilderClick navigates to custom_tarp_builder.html.
4. The custom tarp builder page handles configuration creation and pricing (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: Access favorites from Home
1. User identifies the favorites link or icon in the header.
2. User clicks the favorites access point.
3. onFavoritesClick navigates to favorites.html (or equivalent route), where a separate page handles favorites listing and management using its own interfaces.
interactions:
Interaction: Global search input typing
- Trigger: User types into the global search input field.
- Handler: onInputChange.
- Effect: Debounces user input and optionally calls searchProducts with parameters { query: currentInput, categoryId: undefined, productType: undefined, filters: undefined, sort: undefined, page: 1, pageSize: implementationDefined }.
- UI State: Updates an internal suggestions list with the returned products (if any) without altering the URL. Suggestions can be hidden when input is cleared or loses focus.

Interaction: Global search form submission
- Trigger: User submits the search form (Enter key or search button).
- Handler: onSubmit.
- Preconditions: The search input value is trimmed and validated (e.g., non-empty if a minimum requirement is imposed by the implementation).
- Effect: Constructs a navigation URL catalog.html?query=<encodedSearchTerm>. Navigates to that URL. Does not call searchProducts again specifically for submission on this page (the catalog page is responsible for actual result retrieval).

Interaction: Click on search suggestion
- Trigger: User clicks a suggestion row from the inline search suggestions list.
- Handler: onSuggestionClick.
- Effect: Reads the associated Product.id from the suggestion, then navigates to product.html?id=<Product.id>. Suggestions are dismissed upon navigation.

Interaction: Click on header navigation category link
- Trigger: User clicks a category item in the main navigation.
- Handler: onCategoryClick.
- Effect: Reads Category.id assigned to that link and navigates to catalog.html?categoryId=<Category.id>. No local filter state is retained on Home after navigation (Home is unloaded).

Interaction: Click on homepage category entry point (tile or list item)
- Trigger: User clicks a homepage quick-access category entry.
- Handler: onCategoryTileClick.
- Effect: Reads the Category.id associated with the entry point and navigates to catalog.html?categoryId=<Category.id>.

Interaction: Load featured products on Home
- Trigger: Page initialization (after DOM ready or analogous lifecycle hook).
- Handler: onInit within the featured products component.
- Effect: Calls getHomepageFeaturedProducts (with no parameters). Binds the returned array of Product objects to the featured products UI. Loading and error states are handled internally without affecting the URL.

Interaction: Click featured product card
- Trigger: User clicks a featured product card (or an explicit "View Details" interaction inside the card).
- Handler: onProductClick.
- Effect: Reads Product.id from the clicked card and navigates to product.html?id=<Product.id>.

Interaction: Load header navigation categories and cart summary
- Trigger: Page initialization.
- Handler: onInit within the header-global-nav component.
- Effect: Calls getMainNavigationCategories to populate navigation links and calls getCartSummary to populate the cart indicator. Data is stored in component state and does not modify the URL.

Interaction: Click cart indicator
- Trigger: User clicks the cart icon or label in the header.
- Handler: onCartClick.
- Effect: Navigates to cart.html. The current URL query for Home is not preserved because Home has no functional query state.

Interaction: Hover or click to open mini-cart (optional behavior)
- Trigger: User hovers over or clicks the cart indicator.
- Handler: onCartHover or onCartIndicatorToggle.
- Effect: Toggles visibility of a mini-cart overlay populated from getCartSummary.items and related cart totals. No cart modifications are made on this page.

Interaction: Click custom tarp builder access point
- Trigger: User clicks the "Build Your Custom Tarp" access element in the header or main content.
- Handler: onCustomBuilderClick.
- Effect: Navigates to custom_tarp_builder.html.

Interaction: Click favorites access point
- Trigger: User clicks the favorites icon or text link in the header.
- Handler: onFavoritesClick.
- Effect: Navigates to favorites.html (or equivalent route).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Home page itself does not consume URL parameters; it is always accessed as index.html (with or without irrelevant query string, which the page ignores). Page state is managed via in-memory component state and server responses from the assigned interfaces. URL parameters are used exclusively when navigating away from Home to express state in downstream pages:

- Search navigation: When the user submits the global search form, the page navigates to catalog.html?query=<encodedSearchTerm>. The searchTerm is taken directly from the input value at the time of submission. Home does not read or restore this value from the URL.

- Category navigation: Clicking any category from the header navigation or the homepage category entry component navigates to catalog.html?categoryId=<Category.id>. The Category.id is taken from the Category object returned by getMainNavigationCategories. The set of available IDs is not hardcoded beyond matching the IDs actually present in the API response.

- Featured product navigation: Clicking a featured product card navigates to product.html?id=<Product.id>, where the Product.id comes from the Product object returned by getHomepageFeaturedProducts.

- Custom tarp builder: Clicking the custom builder access point navigates to custom_tarp_builder.html without additional parameters. Any configuration state is handled on the builder page.

- Cart navigation: Clicking the cart indicator navigates to cart.html without additional parameters from Home. Cart identification and state are handled server-side and via interfaces on the cart page.

Home does not store or restore state using URL parameters, localStorage, or other client persistence. All dynamic content (navigation categories, featured products, cart summary, and search suggestions) is obtained via the assigned interfaces on each page load. Any internal UI state (e.g., whether the mini-cart dropdown is open, current search input value, loading/error flags) is kept only in memory and is reset when the user leaves or reloads the page.

components.functionality: Provides global navigation for the site, including logo link, category navigation, links to Home, custom tarp builder, favorites, and a cart indicator. On initialization, it calls getMainNavigationCategories to populate the navigation items and calls getCartSummary to populate the cart indicator state. Category links navigate to the catalog page with categoryId parameters. The cart indicator shows the current total quantity and a price summary derived from the cart object. Clicking the cart indicator navigates to the cart page. The component can optionally display a mini-cart dropdown using the items and totals from getCartSummary, but it does not modify the cart.

Allows users to initiate product searches from any state of the Home page. The component exposes a text input and a submit action. As the user types, onInputChange can call searchProducts with the current query and default or implementation-defined values for other arguments, in order to retrieve a small set of potential matches for inline suggestions. Suggestions are shown in a dropdown without changing the URL. When the user submits the form, onSubmit navigates to catalog.html?query=<encodedSearchTerm>. When a user clicks a suggestion, onSuggestionClick navigates directly to product.html?id=<Product.id>. The component does not retain or use any localStorage state; all search-related state is in memory only.

Displays quick-access entry points for key catalog groupings such as tarps by material, tarps by feature, and protective covers. It consumes the Category list fetched by getMainNavigationCategories (either directly or through shared state with the header). The component filters the available categories to those whose IDs correspond to the key catalog entry points and which are present in the response. For each such category, it renders a clickable item that, when clicked, navigates to catalog.html?categoryId=<Category.id>. If a category is not returned by the API, it is simply not displayed, ensuring behavior remains fully data-driven.

Shows a list or grid of featured products on the Home page. On initialization, it calls getHomepageFeaturedProducts and binds the resulting array of Product entities to the UI. Each product card can display dynamic attributes such as name, size_label, price, and rating based on the Product fields. Clicking any product card triggers navigation to product.html?id=<Product.id>. The component handles loading and error states using only in-memory flags and does not perform any cart modifications or wishlist operations.

Provides a concise view of the current cart status in the header. On initialization, it calls getCartSummary to retrieve the active Cart and associated items, as well as subtotal and estimated_total_before_shipping_and_tax. It binds cart.total_quantity to the cart badge count and binds a total value to a label if desired. Optionally, it can display a mini-cart dropdown listing a subset or all of getCartSummary.items with their quantities and subtotals. The component is read-only on this page; it does not add, remove, or update CartItems. Clicking anywhere on the cart summary (or a dedicated button within it) navigates to cart.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width two-column layout, likely based on an old-school table or float structure rather than a formal modern grid (approx. 960–1000px total width, left column ~25%, right column ~75%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a classic early-2000s catalog layout: a full-width header at the very top containing logo, global navigation, search, and cart summary. Directly beneath, the page splits into a fixed-width two-column structure: a strong left sidebar devoted to category entry points (acting as the primary browsing rail) and a wider right column for task-focused content. In the right column on the home page, the search form appears toward the top as a key starting tool, followed immediately by a vertically flowing area of featured products arranged in a dense grid. The arrangement steers users from brand/nav at the top, to left-side categories for browsing, while the right side provides immediate searching and product discovery.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a classic early-2000s catalog layout: a full-width header at the very top containing logo, global navigation, search, and cart summary. Directly beneath, the page splits into a fixed-width two-column structure: a strong left sidebar devoted to category entry points (acting as the primary browsing rail) and a wider right column for task-focused content. In the right column on the home page, the search form appears toward the top as a key starting tool, followed immediately by a vertically flowing area of featured products arranged in a dense grid. The arrangement steers users from brand/nav at the top, to left-side categories for browsing, while the right side provides immediate searching and product discovery.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header (logo + nav + search + cart) occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height and spans the full page width. Below the header, the left sidebar consumes approximately 25–30% of the fixed content width (around 960–1000px total), stretching almost the full remaining page height with stacked category entries. The main content column on the right occupies the remaining 70–75% of the width. Within this right column, the upper ~30–35% is dedicated to the search form and any brief introductory labeling, while the lower ~65–70% is reserved for a dense featured products grid. Internal paddings and gaps between elements are modest (about 10–16px) for a compact, utilitarian feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is intentionally dense and information-heavy. Navigation elements in both the header and left sidebar are tightly stacked with small vertical spacing and compact fonts. The search form is snugly integrated into the header area or the upper portion of the main column with minimal padding. The featured products grid uses relatively small product tiles with tight spacing between cards and concise text. There is very little open whitespace except around and between product tiles where images need a bit of breathing room to remain legible in the busy layout. Overall, the page prioritizes showing many navigational and product options at once rather than airy minimalism.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the observed Z-shaped pattern. The eye first lands on the top-left area of the header where the logo and main navigation begin, then scans horizontally across the bold horizontal nav bar toward the top-right where the cart summary and possibly the search form reside. From there, attention is pulled downward into the colored left sidebar with stacked category entry points, guiding vertical exploration. After scanning categories, the user’s gaze shifts right into the main content column, first landing on the search area and any top-line copy, then moving downward into the featured products grid. The grid’s images, especially toward the lower-right of the main column, become the final visual anchor, reinforcing a top-left to bottom-right diagonal flow.

layout.overall_description: The home page presents as a fixed-width, early-2000s industrial catalog interface. A full-width header bar caps the top of the page, with a prominent logo on the left and a glossy horizontal navigation band stretching across, accented by a compact cart summary and search zone toward the right. Just below, the layout locks into a two-column structure: a strong, colored left sidebar about one-quarter of the page width runs nearly the full height, packed with vertically stacked category entry points. To its right, a wider main column fills the remaining width, beginning with the search form aligned toward the upper portion, followed by a dense block of featured products arranged in a grid. Margins and paddings are modest, giving the entire page a busy, utilitarian catalog feel. The visual journey moves from top-left logo, across the header nav to the search/cart, down through the left-hand categories, and then across into the right-hand content where the search and featured product images dominate, culminating in the lower-right area of the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global navigation header forms a full-width band fixed to the top of the content area, occupying roughly 15–20% of the viewport height and spanning the entire 960–1000px page width. The left half of this header area is dominated by the company logo and brand mark, aligned to the top-left with minimal padding (around 10–12px from the top and left edges). Directly beneath or partially overlapping the logo area is a bold, glossy horizontal navigation bar in a strong accent color (e.g., red), running from the left margin to near the right margin. Navigation links are arranged side-by-side within this bar, tightly spaced with relatively small font size and minimal horizontal padding, reflecting the dense catalog style. The bar visually anchors the top of the page and serves as a strong horizontal stripe that the eye follows from left to right. On the right side of the header band, the navigation aligns and balances with the cart summary and search: the nav bar visually underlines the header while its rightmost segment visually neighbors the cart summary block to its immediate right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header-global-nav is statically positioned at the top of the page content, not sticky, and remains full-width regardless of scrolling. Its internal layout uses a simple horizontal flow, with the logo anchored to the top-left, the nav bar spanning the width, and reserved space on the top-right for the cart summary and search components to dock.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary sits within the top header region, visually docked to the top-right corner, occupying a compact box roughly 15–20% of the header width and about half the header height. It is aligned along the right margin with about 10–12px padding from the top and right edges. It appears as a small glossy or beveled panel that slightly overlaps or sits just above the right end of the horizontal navigation bar, creating a layered, skeuomorphic feel typical of early-2000s corporate sites. Inside this panel, the cart icon, item count, and a short label (e.g., 'Cart' or 'Your Cart') are tightly packed with small text and minimal vertical spacing. Visually, it acts as a high-density utility box that catches the eye after scanning the navigation bar from left to right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header-cart-summary is statically positioned relative to the header-global-nav, anchored to the top-right within the header area. It does not float independently or stick on scroll; instead, it maintains its relationship with the top navigation bar, appearing as part of the fixed-height header band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search form is integrated into the upper-right portion of the layout, directly beneath the header-cart-summary and aligned along the right edge of the main content width. It occupies roughly 30–40% of the total page width and sits flush with the top of the content area below the header, sharing a horizontal line with the top of the left sidebar. The search field is a long horizontal input spanning most of this width, with a compact search button on its right, both contained within a subtle, glossy panel that echoes the header’s styling. Vertical spacing is tight: only about 8–12px separates the search panel from the bottom of the header above and from the main content below. On the home page, this search zone effectively forms the top band of the right column, visually aligning with the upper rows of the featured products grid that follow underneath. Its placement ensures that after users scan the header and cart, their gaze naturally drops onto the search box as a primary interaction point before moving further down to browse products.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The global-search-form is statically positioned as the topmost element in the right main column, directly under the header region and right-aligned to the main content width. It does not overlay other components; instead, it pushes the featured products grid downward as a fixed-height element with a simple block-level flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category entry points occupy a tall left sidebar that begins immediately below the header and extends downward toward the page footer, consuming roughly 25–30% of the fixed content width. The sidebar is rendered as a strong vertical color block (e.g., a saturated purple or blue) that sharply contrasts with the white or light main content area. Within this sidebar, categories appear as a vertically stacked list of compact tiles or buttons, each spanning nearly the full sidebar width with about 8–10px padding inside and only 4–6px vertical spacing between them. The tiles may have subtle gradients, beveled edges, or glossy tabs to reflect the skeuomorphic aesthetic. The topmost category entries align horizontally with the global-search-form in the right column, creating a balanced upper band across both columns. As users scroll, this column appears as a continuous navigation rail, guiding their eye downward before they naturally shift right into the main content area. The dense stacking, small font size, and limited whitespace communicate that many categories can be scanned quickly in a small area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The homepage-category-entry-points sidebar is a statically positioned left column within the main page body. It does not stick or float; instead, it scrolls with the rest of the content. Its width remains fixed relative to the overall page width (about one-quarter to one-third), and it defines the left boundary against which the right main content column aligns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured products section occupies the majority of the right main content column, positioned directly beneath the global-search-form and spanning the remaining vertical space in the viewport and beyond as the user scrolls. It begins approximately 10–16px below the search panel, with a compact section heading aligned to the top-left of the right column, followed immediately by a dense grid of product tiles. The grid typically uses 3 columns (on a 960–1000px width) within the right column, leaving narrow gutters of about 10–12px between tiles both horizontally and vertically. Each product tile is relatively small and vertically efficient: a product photo dominates the upper portion of the tile, with the name, a one-line description or key spec, and a compact CTA or link stacked below in tight leading. The lower rows extend toward the bottom-right corner, making the images in that area the final focal point in the user’s visual scan. There is minimal extra whitespace: the grid feels full and continuous, creating a catalog-table effect reminiscent of an older, table-based layout. The right and bottom edges of the grid have a bit more empty margin, giving the impression that product imagery has room to breathe along the outer edge, echoing the mockup’s more open area near the product images.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The homepage-featured-products section is a statically flowing block within the right main column. Its top edge is anchored just below the global-search-form, and it expands vertically as more products are loaded, pushing the page length downward. The grid does not reflow responsively in this concept; instead, it maintains a fixed number of columns aligned within the right column, reflecting a traditional fixed-width catalog layout.

name: Product Catalog & Search Results
title: Catalog & Search Results
description: Unified listing page to browse and search industrial tarps and protective covers, apply filters and sorting, view product details, manage favorites, and quickly add items to the cart.

core_features: Display a list of products matching the current URL parameters (query, categoryId, productType, filters, sort, page) using the searchProducts interface.

Provide a contextual header showing whether the user is browsing a category or viewing search results, including the total_results count returned by searchProducts and a summary of applied filters derived from URL parameters.

Expose filter controls (size, exact dimensions, price range, rating, features, shipping options, usage environment, pallet size, pack quantity) based on getCatalogFilterOptions, mapping selections to URL parameters and re-querying via searchProducts.
Allow sorting of results using the available_sorts and applied_sort from searchProducts (e.g., relevance, price ascending/descending, rating descending), bound to the sort URL parameter.
Support pagination by reading the page URL parameter and using total_results and page_size from searchProducts to compute and render next/previous and numbered page navigation.
Enable navigation to individual product detail pages (product.html) when users click product images or titles, passing the Product.id as a URL parameter.
Allow users to add or remove products from favorites directly on product cards by calling addProductToFavorites and removeProductFromFavorites, updating the visual state per card.
Provide a quick add-to-cart action on each product card via addProductToCart, updating an on-page mini-cart summary using the returned cart_summary or a refreshed getCartSummary call.

Render main navigation category links using getMainNavigationCategories so users can switch between catalog sections, updating categoryId (and optionally productType) in the URL when a category is selected.
Show a mini-cart summary in the page header or sidebar using getCartSummary, reflecting current total_quantity, item_count, and subtotal across all pages.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Browse by category
1. User lands on catalog.html with a categoryId parameter set via a navigation link.
2. Page reads categoryId (and any other URL parameters) and constructs a context object for getCatalogFilterOptions.
3. Page calls getCatalogFilterOptions with the context to obtain available filter ranges and facet values.
4. Page builds a filters object from URL parameters (size_label, width_ft, length_ft, min_price, max_price, min_rating, feature, shipping_option, usage_environment, pallet_length_in, pallet_width_in, pack_quantity).
5. Page calls searchProducts with query (if present), categoryId, productType (if present), filters, sort, page, and pageSize.
6. Page renders the catalog-context header, filter-sidebar, sort-bar, and product-grid based on returned data.
7. User adjusts filters (e.g., selects a material size_label or a feature checkbox); the page updates the URL parameters accordingly and re-executes getCatalogFilterOptions and searchProducts.
8. User changes the sort order or navigates to another pagination page; the URL is updated (sort or page), and searchProducts is called again to refresh the list.

Workflow: Search by keyword
1. User enters a keyword in the global search bar on index.html and submits.
2. index.html navigates to catalog.html with a query parameter (and optionally productType or categoryId if provided).
3. catalog.html reads query and other URL parameters, then calls getCatalogFilterOptions with a context built from these parameters.
4. Page builds a filters object from current URL parameters and calls searchProducts with query, categoryId, productType, filters, sort, page, and pageSize.
5. The catalog-context header displays a search-focused message (e.g., showing the query and total_results).
6. User refines results using filters or sort; each refinement updates corresponding URL parameters and causes searchProducts to be called again.
7. User can further refine the query using an in-page search refine form that updates the query URL parameter and resets page to the first page before calling searchProducts.

Workflow: Find pallet covers by pallet size and pack quantity
1. User opens catalog.html with categoryId set to a pallet-related category or navigates to it via the main navigation, which sets categoryId in the URL.
2. Page calls getCatalogFilterOptions with this context and receives pallet_sizes and pack_quantities options.
3. User clicks a pallet size facet option; the page updates pallet_length_in and pallet_width_in URL parameters to match the selected size and resets page to the first page.
4. Page reconstructs the filters object with the new pallet_length_in and pallet_width_in values and calls searchProducts.
5. User optionally selects a pack quantity facet; pack_quantity is added to the URL parameters and included in filters on the next searchProducts call.
6. User navigates to specific products by clicking product cards or refines further via price, rating, or feature filters.

Workflow: Add products to favorites from results
1. User reviews product cards in the product-grid.
2. On a card, user clicks the favorites toggle control.
3. If the product is not currently marked as favorited in the local UI state, the page calls addProductToFavorites with the Product.id.
4. On a successful response (success = true), the page updates the card’s favorite UI state to indicate that the product is favorited.
5. If the product is already marked as favorited and the user clicks the toggle, the page calls removeProductFromFavorites with the Product.id.
6. On a successful response, the page updates the card’s favorite UI state to the non-favorited state, without changing URL parameters.

Workflow: Quick add-to-cart from product cards
1. User locates a product in the product-grid and confirms its key attributes (size_label, material, price) as displayed on the card.
2. User clicks a quick add-to-cart button on the card.
3. Page calls addProductToCart with the productId and a default or user-selected quantity.
4. On success, the page reads cart_summary from the response and updates the mini-cart-summary component with new total_quantity, item_count, and subtotal; optionally, the page may also call getCartSummary to fully refresh cart details.
5. User can continue browsing; the URL parameters remain unchanged by the add-to-cart action.
6. If the add to cart fails (success = false), the page surfaces the message from addProductToCart without changing the catalog results.

Workflow: Navigate to product details and back
1. User finds a product of interest in the product-grid.
2. User clicks the product image or name link.
3. Browser navigates to product.html with id set to the selected Product.id.
4. User reviews product details and then uses the browser back button (or a link) to return to catalog.html.
5. Because the full search state is encoded in URL parameters (query, categoryId, filters, sort, page), the catalog.html page reconstructs filters and calls searchProducts and getCatalogFilterOptions so the user sees the same listing context as before navigating.

Workflow: Use main navigation to switch catalog sections
1. Page loads or user is on catalog.html and sees the main navigation built using getMainNavigationCategories.
2. User clicks a category navigation item.
3. The site navigates to catalog.html with categoryId set to the selected category’s id and optionally adjusts productType according to navigation rules.
4. catalog.html reads the updated URL parameters, clears any filter parameters that are not applicable (logic defined by the app), and calls getCatalogFilterOptions and searchProducts for the new category context.
5. The catalog-context header updates to reflect the selected category and the product-grid shows only products related to that category.
interactions: Selecting or deselecting any filter option (size, dimensions, price range, rating threshold, feature, shipping option, usage environment, pallet size, pack quantity) updates the corresponding URL parameters and triggers a new searchProducts call with an updated filters object.
Changing the sort control updates the sort URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and calls searchProducts with the new sort value.
Using pagination controls (next, previous, specific page number) updates the page URL parameter and calls searchProducts while keeping all other URL parameters unchanged.
Submitting an in-page search refine form updates the query URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and calls searchProducts with the new query.

Clicking the 'clear all filters' control removes all filter-related URL parameters while preserving query, categoryId, and productType, then calls searchProducts and getCatalogFilterOptions for the cleared state.
Clicking a single applied-filter pill removes only that filter’s parameter from the URL and re-calls searchProducts with the remaining filters.
Clicking a product card image or title navigates the browser to product.html with the Product.id passed as the id URL parameter.
Clicking the favorites toggle on a product card triggers either addProductToFavorites or removeProductFromFavorites for that product and updates only the local UI favorite state for that card.

Clicking the quick add-to-cart button on a product card calls addProductToCart and then updates the mini-cart-summary using the returned cart_summary data; it does not modify the catalog URL parameters.
Loading the page or refreshing it triggers getCartSummary to populate the mini-cart-summary and getMainNavigationCategories to populate navigation, without changing existing catalog URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s primary state is fully represented by URL parameters so that navigation, refresh, and back/forward browser actions always restore the user’s exact catalog context.

On initial load, catalog.html parses all defined incoming URL parameters: query, categoryId, productType, size_label, width_ft, length_ft, min_price, max_price, min_rating, feature, shipping_option, usage_environment, pallet_length_in, pallet_width_in, pack_quantity, sort, and page. Any parameter not present in the URL is treated as undefined and not included in filter or sort logic.

The page constructs a context object for getCatalogFilterOptions from the available high-level parameters (typically query, categoryId, and productType). The response from getCatalogFilterOptions defines the filter options that should be displayed; filter UI components must only present options derived from this response and should mark options as selected based on matching URL parameter values.

For searchProducts, the page builds a filters object using only the URL parameters that represent filters (size_label, width_ft, length_ft, min_price, max_price, min_rating, feature, shipping_option, usage_environment, pallet_length_in, pallet_width_in, pack_quantity). If a given parameter is absent or invalid, it is omitted from the filters object. searchProducts is called with query, categoryId, productType, filters, sort, page, and pageSize. page is derived from the URL (with a default of the first page if not present or invalid). pageSize is determined by application configuration or back-end defaults and is not controlled through URL parameters on this page.

Whenever the user interacts with filter controls, sort options, pagination, or the in-page search refine form, the page updates the URL parameters using standard browser navigation mechanisms (query string updates via full-page reload or history state updates). After updating the URL, the page re-runs getCatalogFilterOptions (when necessary for the new context) and searchProducts with the latest parameters. All components (context header, filter-sidebar, product-grid, sort-bar, pagination-controls) read from the same source of truth: the current URL and the latest interface responses.

Actions that do not affect the catalog state, such as addProductToFavorites, removeProductFromFavorites, addProductToCart, getCartSummary, and getMainNavigationCategories, do not modify URL parameters. Their results are kept in transient in-memory UI state within the page’s components. No catalog-related state is stored in localStorage or similar persistent client-side storage; the URL alone governs catalog state.

components.functionality: Retrieves and displays the main navigation categories so users can move between major catalog sections. Selecting a category navigates to catalog.html with categoryId (and optionally productType) set in the URL.
event_handlers: onLoad: Call getMainNavigationCategories with no parameters and render navigation links for each returned Category.

onCategoryClick: Read the clicked Category.id, construct a catalog.html URL with categoryId set to this id (preserving or resetting other parameters according to navigation rules), and navigate the browser to that URL.

components.functionality: Shows the current catalog context (category name or search query), the number of matching products, and a summary of applied filters. Provides actions to clear all filters or remove individual filters.
event_handlers: onLoadOrUrlChange: Read query, categoryId, productType, and filter parameters from the URL. Use categoryId to match a Category from the main navigation data (if available) and use searchProducts.total_results to show how many items match.

onRenderAppliedFilters: For each filter-related URL parameter (size_label, width_ft, length_ft, min_price, max_price, min_rating, feature, shipping_option, usage_environment, pallet_length_in, pallet_width_in, pack_quantity), render a removable pill if the parameter is present.

onClearAllFiltersClick: Remove all filter-related parameters from the URL while preserving query, categoryId, productType, and sort; reset page to the first page; update the browser URL accordingly; then re-trigger searchProducts and getCatalogFilterOptions.

onRemoveSingleFilterClick: Remove only the corresponding filter parameter from the URL, reset page to the first page, update the URL, then re-run searchProducts (and getCatalogFilterOptions if the removed filter might change available options).

components.functionality: Allows users to refine the current keyword search within the catalog.html page by updating the query URL parameter and re-running the product search.
event_handlers: onLoadOrUrlChange: Pre-fill the search input with the current query parameter value from the URL, if any.

onSubmit: Prevent default form submission, read the current text input value, update the query parameter in the URL (or remove it if empty), reset page to the first page, and re-call searchProducts with the new query and existing non-search parameters.

components.functionality: Displays all available catalog filters (size, dimensions, price, rating, features, shipping, usage environment, pallet size, pack quantity) based on the getCatalogFilterOptions response. Synchronizes filter selections with URL parameters and triggers new searches.
event_handlers: onLoadOrUrlChange: Build a context from query, categoryId, and productType URL parameters and call getCatalogFilterOptions(context). Render controls for price, size_options, rating_thresholds, feature_options, shipping_options, usage_environments, pallet_sizes, and pack_quantities. Initialize each control’s selected state based on matching URL parameters.

onPriceChange: When the user adjusts price controls, validate the new minimum and maximum, update min_price and max_price URL parameters (removing them if cleared), reset page to the first page, then call searchProducts with updated filters.

onSizeOptionSelect: When the user selects a size_options item, update size_label (and optionally width_ft and length_ft if applicable) in the URL, reset page to the first page, and call searchProducts with new filters.

onDimensionInputChange: If the user inputs explicit width_ft or length_ft, update those parameters in the URL (clearing size_label if defined by a discrete option as needed), reset page to the first page, and re-call searchProducts.
onRatingThresholdSelect: When the user selects a rating threshold, update min_rating in the URL to the corresponding value, reset page, and execute searchProducts.

onFeatureToggle: When a feature option is toggled, update the feature parameter in the URL based on the current selection rules (e.g., a single feature key or an updated value), reset page to the first page, and call searchProducts.
onShippingOptionToggle: When a shipping option is toggled, update shipping_option in the URL, reset page, and call searchProducts.
onUsageEnvironmentSelect: When a usage environment is selected, update usage_environment in the URL and call searchProducts with page reset.

onPalletSizeSelect: When a pallet size option is selected, set pallet_length_in and pallet_width_in in the URL from the selected pallet_sizes item, reset page to the first page, and call searchProducts (this also covers the specific case of selecting a particular pallet size facet).
onPackQuantitySelect: When a pack quantity is selected, update pack_quantity in the URL, reset page, and re-call searchProducts.

components.functionality: Provides a dropdown or set of controls to change the sort order of catalog results using the available_sorts and applied_sort from searchProducts.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsLoad: Read applied_sort and available_sorts from the latest searchProducts response and populate the sort controls, marking the applied_sort as selected.

onSortChange: When the user selects a new sort option, update the sort URL parameter, reset page to the first page, then call searchProducts with the new sort value and the same query, categoryId, productType, and filters derived from URL parameters.

components.functionality: Renders the list or grid of products returned by searchProducts, delegating per-item functionality to product-card components and showing an empty state when no products are found.
event_handlers: onLoadOrUrlChange: Build the filters object from filter-related URL parameters and call searchProducts with query, categoryId, productType, filters, sort, page, and pageSize. Use the returned products array and pagination metadata to render product cards and provide data for the pagination-controls component.
onEmptyResults: If searchProducts.total_results is zero, show an appropriate empty-state message and keep filter and sort controls available for further refinement.

components.functionality: Displays a single product’s key information (name, size, material, price, rating, shipping flags, etc.), supports navigation to the product detail page, toggling favorites, and quick add-to-cart.
event_handlers: onImageOrTitleClick: Read the Product.id for this card and navigate to product.html with id set to this value in the URL.

onFavoriteToggleClick: If the card is not currently marked as favorited in the UI, call addProductToFavorites with this Product.id. On success, update the card’s local favorite state so it appears as favorited. If the card is currently marked as favorited, call removeProductFromFavorites with this Product.id and, on success, update local state to non-favorited. This does not change URL parameters.

onQuickAddToCartClick: Determine the desired quantity (from a quantity control on the card or an implicit default) and call addProductToCart with Product.id and that quantity. On success, use the returned cart_summary to update the mini-cart-summary component. On failure, surface the message from addProductToCart in the card or a global notification without changing catalog state.

components.functionality: Lets users navigate between pages of search results by updating the page URL parameter and re-calling searchProducts.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsLoad: Use searchProducts.page, searchProducts.page_size, and searchProducts.total_results to calculate the total number of pages and which pagination controls to display.

onPageNumberClick: When a specific page number is selected, update the page URL parameter to that number (keeping query, categoryId, productType, filters, and sort unchanged) and call searchProducts with the updated page.
onNextPageClick: If a next page exists, increment the page URL parameter by one and call searchProducts.
onPreviousPageClick: If a previous page exists, decrement the page URL parameter by one and call searchProducts.

components.functionality: Shows a compact summary of the current cart, including total_quantity, item_count, and subtotal, and updates in response to add-to-cart actions.
event_handlers: onLoadOrUrlChange: Call getCartSummary to retrieve the current cart and items, then render total quantities and subtotal.

onAddToCartSuccess: When an addProductToCart call from any product-card completes successfully, read the returned cart_summary and update the displayed totals immediately. Optionally, re-call getCartSummary if a full refresh of item details is needed.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width two-column layout, likely based on an old-school table or float structure rather than a formal modern grid (approx. 960–1000px total width, left column ~25%, right column ~75%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a classic early-2000s catalog layout: global site header and horizontal navigation at the very top (outside the scope of the listed components), then a two-column body. The left column is a continuous vertical strip combining main-navigation-categories at the top and the filter-sidebar below. The right column is the working area for the catalog: at the top, the catalog-context-header and mini-cart-summary share a narrow header band; immediately beneath that, the search-refine-form spans the full width of the right column; directly below comes a slim sort-bar; and beneath that the product-grid fills most of the vertical space, ending with pagination-controls at the bottom. Content flows in a linear top-to-bottom manner within each column, with a strong emphasis on the left column for exploration and the right column for results.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a classic early-2000s catalog layout: global site header and horizontal navigation at the very top (outside the scope of the listed components), then a two-column body. The left column is a continuous vertical strip combining main-navigation-categories at the top and the filter-sidebar below. The right column is the working area for the catalog: at the top, the catalog-context-header and mini-cart-summary share a narrow header band; immediately beneath that, the search-refine-form spans the full width of the right column; directly below comes a slim sort-bar; and beneath that the product-grid fills most of the vertical space, ending with pagination-controls at the bottom. Content flows in a linear top-to-bottom manner within each column, with a strong emphasis on the left column for exploration and the right column for results.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The entire layout sits in a fixed-width container approximately 960–1000px wide, centered in the browser. The top header (logo + global nav) takes about 15–20% of the viewport height but is assumed to be shared across the site. Below that, the two-column body uses roughly 25–30% of the width for the left sidebar and 70–75% for the right main content. Within the left sidebar, the upper ~35–40% is dedicated to main-navigation-categories and the remaining ~60–65% to the filter-sidebar. In the right column, the combined band of catalog-context-header and mini-cart-summary occupies about 10–15% of the right-column height above the fold, the search-refine-form another ~5–8%, the sort-bar a slim ~3–5%, and the product-grid dominates the remaining 65–75% of the main column height, with pagination-controls occupying a shallow horizontal strip at the bottom. Padding inside panels is modest (around 8–12px) to maintain a compact, utilitarian feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is intentionally high, echoing early-2000s corporate catalogs: navigation links in the left column are stacked with tight line spacing and minimal vertical gaps. Filter controls (checkboxes, dropdowns) are closely grouped, with small labels and little whitespace between fields. The catalog-context-header uses a slightly larger font for the title but retains tight leading. The search-refine-form and sort-bar are compact horizontal bands with small input controls placed close together. The product-grid arranges product-card boxes with narrow gutters (8–12px) and minimal internal padding, fitting two to three cards per row depending on viewport width within the fixed container. The only comparatively open area is the white space around each product-card image inside the grid, providing a subtle visual rest while still feeling dense overall.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first goes to the global logo and main site nav (outside these components), then quickly to the strong horizontal band at the top of the catalog area formed by the catalog-context-header and the mini-cart-summary on the right. From there, the eye is pulled down the saturated left sidebar, moving through the main-navigation-categories and then into the filter-sidebar blocks. After scanning the left column, the user’s focus shifts right to the search-refine-form and sort-bar, then flows downward into the product-grid. Within the grid, attention zigzags across rows of product-cards, with each card’s product image and 'Add to Cart' area drawing the final focus. The overall path is a Z-shaped movement: top-left global branding, across the top to the right-column header and mini-cart, down the left sidebar, then across into the main content and down through the product-grid.

layout.overall_description: The catalog page sits inside a fixed-width, centered frame reminiscent of early-2000s corporate sites, with a glossy top header and a colored horizontal navigation bar above the main body. Immediately below, the page splits into two clear columns. On the left, a tall, colored sidebar runs nearly the full height of the page, starting with a dense vertical list of product categories and continuing into stacked filter panels; this strip feels like a continuous control panel for narrowing the product set. On the right, a white main content panel holds the active catalog view. At the top of this panel, a narrow header band shows the catalog title and context on the left and a compact mini-cart box on the right, both framed by subtle gradients and thin borders. Just beneath, a slim search-refine bar stretches across the column, followed by an equally trim sort bar. Below these narrow bands, the main area opens into a tight but orderly grid of product cards, arranged in multiple columns with small gutters and minimal padding, giving a sense of a dense industrial catalog. At the very bottom, centered pagination controls form a final horizontal strip. The overall impression is utilitarian and information-heavy, with a strong left-hand navigation spine and a right-hand content panel that feels like a catalog page pinned next to a control sidebar, guiding the eye from the navigation, through the filters, and into the product results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main-navigation-categories component occupies the upper portion of the left sidebar column, starting directly beneath the global header and spanning the full width of the left column (approximately 25–30% of the total page width). It sits flush against the left edge of the page’s content area, with a modest 10–12px padding on the right and inner sides. Vertically, it claims roughly the top 35–40% of the sidebar’s height, forming a tall block with a strong colored or gradient background that matches the site’s sidebar styling (e.g., a purple-toned panel). Within this block, category links are arranged as a tight vertical list: each item is a full-width row, left-aligned, with minimal vertical spacing (4–6px) between lines and small font sizes, giving a compact, menu-like appearance. A simple header label such as “Product Categories” can appear at the very top of this block, perhaps in a slightly darker gradient subheader strip. The bottom edge of this component sits directly above the filter-sidebar, with just a thin separator line or a 4–6px gap, maintaining the impression of a continuous sidebar column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element anchored to the top of the left sidebar; it scrolls with the page and does not stick or float. Its width is fixed by the left column, and its height is determined by the number of categories but visually treated as the upper segment of a continuous sidebar panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The catalog-context-header sits at the top of the right main content column, immediately under the global site header and horizontally aligned with the top of the left sidebar. It spans roughly 70–75% of the right column’s width, anchored to the left of that column, leaving space at its right edge for the mini-cart-summary. The component appears as a shallow horizontal band, about 60–80px tall, with a subtle glossy gradient or lightly shaded background differentiating it from the white content area below. Inside, the main catalog title (e.g., “Industrial Tarps Catalog”) is left-aligned and sits near the top-left corner of this band, with tightly spaced subtitle or context text (e.g., “Showing tarps in Heavy-Duty category”) directly beneath. Padding inside the header band is modest (around 10–12px on all sides). The bottom of this header band touches the top edge of the search-refine-form with a thin dividing line, reinforcing the stacked, modular look within the main content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky top band of the right column. It always appears above the search and product-grid, and scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-refine-form is positioned directly below the catalog-context-header, spanning the full width of the right main content column (approximately 70–75% of total page width) from the inner edge of the left sidebar to the right margin of the content area. It appears as a narrow horizontal panel, about 50–60px tall, with a lightly tinted background and a thin top border (aligned under the catalog-context-header) and bottom border (aligned above the sort-bar), creating a clear banded structure. Within this band, the primary search field (keyword search) is left-aligned and occupies about 60% of the panel’s width, with a compact text input box and a small, rectangular search button placed immediately to its right, using tight spacing (4–6px gaps). Any additional refine options (e.g., a dropdown for 'Search within results') are arranged inline to the right of the main search input or stacked in a second line if space is tight, but still within the same band. Vertical padding above and below the inputs is minimal (8–10px), keeping the panel compact. The right edge of the panel lines up with the right edge of the mini-cart-summary above, preserving a neat column alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band between the catalog-context-header and sort-bar. It does not stick to the top on scroll; it scrolls away with the rest of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar occupies the lower portion of the left column, sitting directly beneath the main-navigation-categories and extending down to near the bottom of the page content. It shares the same overall width as the categories (about 25–30% of the total page width) and visually appears as a continuation of the left sidebar, often sharing the same colored or gradient background. The top of this component starts either flush to the bottom of the main-navigation-categories block or separated by a very thin rule and 4–8px vertical gap. Within this area, filter groups (e.g., Material, Size, Color, Thickness) are arranged as stacked panels. Each group has a small, bold group header bar occupying the full width of the sidebar with 6–8px top and bottom padding, followed by a tightly packed list of checkboxes, radio buttons, or dropdowns. Each filter option has minimal vertical space (2–4px) between lines to maintain high density. Horizontal padding from the left border is around 10px, creating a slight inset for controls. The component extends downward as long as needed; in many cases, it will be taller than the viewport, encouraging vertical scrolling in the main page, with filters always anchored to the left side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical block that scrolls with the page. It is visually fixed to the left edge but does not use sticky behavior; as users scroll down through the product-grid on the right, the filter-sidebar moves up and out of view proportionally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-bar is a slim horizontal strip directly beneath the search-refine-form and immediately above the product-grid, spanning the full width of the right column. Its height is shallow—approximately 35–45px—giving it the feel of a control ribbon. The background is slightly darker or more metallic-looking than the search-refine-form, echoing an early-2000s toolbar with a subtle gradient and possibly a thin inner shadow at the top. Inside, the left side of the bar (about 50–60% of its width) contains small, tightly spaced informational text like “Showing 1–24 of 128 results” and view mode toggles (e.g., tiny icon buttons for grid/list) arranged inline with minimal spacing. On the right side (about 40–50% of the width), the sort dropdown (e.g., “Sort by: Relevance”) is right-aligned, with its label and control packed closely together. Padding above and below the text is modest (6–8px), and horizontal margins between elements are kept to about 4–6px, emphasizing compactness. The bottom of the sort-bar has a subtle border that defines the top edge of the product-grid area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal control bar; it stays attached above the product-grid and scrolls away along with the grid content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid occupies the main bulk of the right column, starting immediately below the sort-bar and extending down to just above the pagination-controls. It spans the full right-column width (approximately 70–75% of the total page width), with small internal gutters and a white or very light background to contrast the denser, colored controls above. Inside the grid, product-card components are laid out in a multi-column, row-based fashion reminiscent of early catalog pages: three cards per row where width allows (each card taking roughly 30–31% of the grid width), with narrow horizontal gaps (8–12px) between cards and a similar vertical gap (10–14px) between rows. The grid aligns its first column directly under the left edge of the catalog-context-header, and its rightmost cards align under the right edge of the mini-cart and search bar, preserving a crisp column. The entire grid area feels dense but orderly, with no large empty spaces; when there are fewer items, cards are aligned to the left, leaving empty space on the right rather than centering them. The grid area grows vertically as needed, pushing the pagination-controls further down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive within the fixed-width container: the number of columns (typically two or three) may adjust slightly with viewport width, but within the given fixed width it remains a standard multi-column grid that scrolls vertically with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each product-card is a compact, boxy tile within the product-grid, approximately one-third of the grid’s width (30–31%) and a fixed height that supports a dense but legible arrangement of content. The card has a thin border and a subtle drop shadow or beveled edge to evoke the early-2000s boxed look, set against the white grid background. Inside the card, content is organized top-to-bottom: a narrow title bar at the very top spans the full width of the card, with a slightly darker gradient background and the product name in a small bold font, tightly padded (6–8px). Below this, on the upper-left half of the card, a small product thumbnail image is placed, occupying roughly 50–60% of the card width and about 40% of its height, with a bit of white space around it. To the right of the image or directly beneath it (depending on card width), a short, tightly spaced bullet list or spec table lists key attributes such as dimensions, material, and weight, using small fonts and minimal line spacing. At the bottom-right corner of the card, an 'Add to Cart' button and possibly a small 'Favorite' icon are placed; the button is rectangular with a glossy gradient, sized to be noticeable but still compact, and often aligned to the card’s right edge to create the classic top-left to bottom-right emphasis. Price and availability line(s) sit just above this button, left-aligned but close to the bottom, leaving only a narrow margin (8–10px) between the lowest elements and the card border. Overall internal padding is tight—around 8–10px on each side—reinforcing the dense catalog feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static tile within the product-grid. Its dimensions are consistent across the grid row, and it does not expand or collapse dynamically; hover states might slightly highlight the border or background but do not alter the basic layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls component is located at the bottom of the right main content column, directly beneath the last row of the product-grid. It occupies a shallow band, roughly 40–50px tall, spanning about 60–70% of the right column’s width and centered horizontally within it. The background is either white or lightly tinted to subtly distinguish it from the grid above, with a thin top border marking the separation. Inside, pagination elements (Previous/Next links, numeric page buttons) are laid out in a single horizontal line, centered, with small but clearly clickable boxes or text links. The spacing between page numbers is modest (6–8px), maintaining the dense aesthetic without feeling cramped. A brief “Page X of Y” text may sit to the left of the numeric buttons or integrated as part of the control string, all on a single line. There is a small vertical margin (8–12px) above and below the controls to avoid them touching directly against the grid and the bottom edge of the content area, but whitespace remains relatively tight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer-like control within the main content area. It scrolls into view as the user reaches the bottom of the product-grid and does not remain fixed on screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mini-cart-summary is placed in the upper-right portion of the right main content column, sharing the top band with the catalog-context-header but occupying the rightmost 25–30% of that band. While the catalog-context-header starts at the left edge of the right column, the mini-cart-summary is docked to the right edge, creating a two-part header strip. The component appears as a small boxed widget with a beveled or slightly raised border and a subtle gradient background, drawing attention without overshadowing the main catalog title. Its height aligns with or slightly exceeds that of the catalog-context-header, usually around 60–80px. Inside, a compact cart icon and a 'Cart: X items' label are right-aligned near the top of the box, with the current subtotal displayed just beneath in a slightly bolder or colored font. A small 'View Cart' or 'Checkout' link/button sits at the bottom-right corner of the widget, with minimal vertical spacing (4–6px) separating lines of text. Padding within the mini-cart is tight (8–10px), and the left edge of the mini-cart aligns vertically with the rightmost edge of the catalog-context-header content, so together they form a continuous, visually unified top band for the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static panel anchored to the top-right of the right column. It does not float or stay sticky; as the user scrolls down through the results, the mini-cart-summary scrolls out of view along with the catalog-context-header.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a single tarp or protective cover, including size, material, thickness, features, ratings, pack options, and controls to add the item to the cart or favorites.

core_features: Load a single product by URL parameter id using getProductDetails and display all available attributes (size, material, thickness, features, usage environment, pallet compatibility, pack quantity, price, rating, and image).
Display pack options returned by getProductDetails.pack_options and allow the user to switch between them for price and pack-size context on the page.
Provide a quantity input and an Add to Cart action that calls addProductToCart with the current productId and quantity, and then surfaces updated cart totals.
Display cart status (item count, subtotal) by using getCartSummary on page load and refreshing it with cart_summary returned from addProductToCart.
Allow the user to add the product to favorites via addProductToFavorites and remove it from favorites via removeProductFromFavorites, with a single toggle control.
Display rating information (average_rating and rating_count) and basic shipping indicators derived from product fields (e.g., has_free_shipping).
Offer navigation controls to return to the pallet covers catalog for pallet_cover products and to open the cart page after adding items.
user_workflows: View product details: 1) User navigates to product.html?id={productId}. 2) Page reads the id parameter and calls getProductDetails(productId). 3) While loading, page shows a loading state; on success, the product data and pack_options populate the header, image, specs, pricing, and pack option components. 4) Simultaneously, getCartSummary is called to populate the mini-cart indicator.

Select pack option: 1) User sees a list or selector of pack_options returned from getProductDetails. 2) User clicks a pack option. 3) Page updates local selectedPackOption state (based on the clicked option) and uses this to adjust displayed price, unit breakdown, and labels around the quantity field. 4) The parameters sent to addProductToCart remain productId and quantity; the backend determines pricing for the selected product based on its own configuration.

Adjust quantity and add to cart: 1) User enters a quantity value in the quantity field. 2) Client validates the value (e.g., positive integer). 3) User submits the Add to Cart form. 4) Page calls addProductToCart with productId from the current product and the entered quantity. 5) On success, page updates the mini-cart indicator using the cart_summary in the response and may show a confirmation message. 6) If the user clicks View Cart in that confirmation or header, browser navigates to cart.html.

Toggle favorites: 1) On initial load, the favorites toggle defaults to an unselected state (because the page does not have a favorites-read interface). 2) When user clicks the favorites toggle in unselected state, the page calls addProductToFavorites(productId). 3) If the call succeeds, the component marks the product as favorited for the current session. If the call fails with a message indicating it is already in favorites, the component also interprets this as favorited. 4) When the toggle is in selected state and user clicks again, the page calls removeProductFromFavorites(productId). 5) On success, the component marks the product as not favorited.

Navigate back to catalog: 1) If the loaded product has product_type = pallet_cover, the page shows a breadcrumb or link labeled to go back to pallet covers. 2) When clicked, browser navigates to catalog.html?categoryId=pallet_covers. 3) For products of other types, the page can show a generic back-to-catalog link that navigates to catalog.html without additional parameters.

Inspect ratings and features: 1) User views rating summary (average_rating and rating_count) and feature flags (waterproof, fire-retardant, UV-resistant, heavy-duty) in the specs panel. 2) No additional API calls are required; this information is read directly from the product response returned by getProductDetails.
interactions: On page load, read the id URL parameter and trigger getProductDetails and getCartSummary; render corresponding loading, success, or error states.
Click on a pack option to change the selected pack option; update displayed price, pack-size labels, and any per-unit calculations based on the selected option.
Change the quantity input value; validate it and update any derived totals shown in the pricing section before submission.

Submit the Add to Cart form (button click or form submission); call addProductToCart with productId and quantity; handle success (update cart indicator and confirmation UI) and error (show validation or failure message).
Click the favorites toggle button; depending on current local state, call addProductToFavorites or removeProductFromFavorites; update the toggle’s visual state based on the response.
Click the mini-cart or View Cart button after a successful add; navigate to cart.html.
Click the Back to Pallet Covers link for pallet_cover products; navigate to catalog.html?categoryId=pallet_covers.

Click product image thumbnail(s) or main image container (if multiple images are available from image_url variations derived on the client); update the main displayed image without additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the URL parameter id. When product.html is loaded, the client reads id from the query string (e.g., product.html?id={productId}). If id is missing or empty, the page shows an error state instructing the user to navigate back to the catalog. If id is present, the page calls getProductDetails(id). The product and pack_options returned become the base state for all other components. The selectedPackOption state is initialized from the pack_options array (preferring the element where is_selected is true; if none, the first element is used). This selectedPackOption is maintained as in-memory page state and does not modify the URL. The quantity state is also maintained in-memory and resets whenever id changes (i.e., when the user navigates to a different product via a new product.html?id=...). Cart state for the header indicator is initially loaded from getCartSummary and then locally updated using the cart_summary returned by addProductToCart. Favorites state is local to the page session and updated based on responses from addProductToFavorites and removeProductFromFavorites. Outgoing navigation does not carry product detail state beyond what is defined: (1) navigating to cart.html uses no additional parameters; (2) navigating back to pallet covers uses catalog.html?categoryId=pallet_covers as specified; (3) if the user moves from one product to another (e.g., via internal navigation), the page updates the id parameter in the URL and re-runs the load logic.

components.functionality: Reads the id URL parameter, calls getProductDetails with that productId, and exposes the loaded product and pack_options to the rest of the page. Handles loading and error states. Also calls getCartSummary on initial load to populate the mini-cart indicator.

Displays navigation breadcrumbs based on the loaded product. For pallet_cover products, it shows a Back to Pallet Covers link that navigates to the pallet covers catalog. For other product types, it may show a generic link back to the main catalog.
Shows the product name, SKU (if available), size label, color, and a compact rating summary. Includes the favorites toggle button that allows the user to add or remove the product from favorites.

Manages favorites state for the current product. Starts in a default unselected state, calls addProductToFavorites on first click to add the product to favorites, and switches to selected state on success. When in selected state, calls removeProductFromFavorites on click and switches back to unselected state on success. Interprets an addProductToFavorites response indicating the product is already in favorites as a signal to set the UI to selected.

Displays the primary product image using image_url from the product. If the implementation derives multiple image variants from this URL, it allows the user to switch between them, updating the main displayed image without additional API calls.

Shows technical details of the product using available fields: size (width_ft, length_ft, size_label), material, color, thickness (thickness_value and thickness_unit), feature flags (heavy duty, waterproof, fire retardant, UV resistant), usage_environment, interior dimensions for covers, pallet dimensions and pack_quantity for pallet covers, and any descriptive text. This component is read-only and purely data-driven from getProductDetails.

Displays the product's average star rating and rating_count. Shows a non-interactive rating visualization (e.g., stars) and a rating count label. All data is read from the product returned by getProductDetails; no rating submission is performed on this page.

Displays basic shipping information based on product.has_free_shipping. If has_free_shipping is true, shows an indicator for free shipping; otherwise, shows a generic note that shipping will be calculated during checkout. This component does not call any shipping-related APIs.

Displays pack options for the product when getProductDetails returns pack_options. Each option shows its pack_quantity and price. One option is marked as selected based on is_selected, with a fallback to the first option if none is marked. When the user selects a different pack option, the component updates local selectedPackOption state and notifies the pricing and quantity form to refresh displayed price and labels. No additional API calls are made when switching options.

Shows current price and any per-pack or per-unit calculations derived from Product.price and the currently selected pack option. Provides a quantity input (number of packs or units depending on business rules) and an Add to Cart button. On submission, it validates quantity, then calls addProductToCart with product.id and the chosen quantity. On success, it uses the cart_summary in the response to update the mini-cart indicator and may trigger a confirmation message; on error, it displays an appropriate error state.

Displays a compact summary of the cart, such as total_quantity and subtotal. Initially populated via getCartSummary when the page loads, and subsequently updated using the cart_summary returned from addProductToCart. Provides a View Cart action that navigates the user to cart.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width two-column layout, likely based on an old-school table or float structure rather than a formal modern grid (approx. 960–1000px total width, left column ~25%, right column ~75%).
layout.layout_pattern: Use a classic early-2000s corporate layout: a fixed-width page (~960–1000px) centered in the viewport. At the top, a full-width header contains logo and a glossy horizontal navigation bar; the mini-cart indicator sits on the right end of this nav. Below the header, the page splits into two columns: a tall, tinted left sidebar for category navigation (out of scope) and a main content column on the right where all product detail components live. Inside the right column, content flows top-to-bottom: breadcrumb, then a fairly dense info band (title, ratings, shipping, specs, options, and add-to-cart block), and finally a large product image anchored toward the bottom-right. The arrangement emphasizes a diagonal reading from top-left text to bottom-right image.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a classic early-2000s corporate layout: a fixed-width page (~960–1000px) centered in the viewport. At the top, a full-width header contains logo and a glossy horizontal navigation bar; the mini-cart indicator sits on the right end of this nav. Below the header, the page splits into two columns: a tall, tinted left sidebar for category navigation (out of scope) and a main content column on the right where all product detail components live. Inside the right column, content flows top-to-bottom: breadcrumb, then a fairly dense info band (title, ratings, shipping, specs, options, and add-to-cart block), and finally a large product image anchored toward the bottom-right. The arrangement emphasizes a diagonal reading from top-left text to bottom-right image.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate the header (logo + nav + mini-cart indicator) to the top ~15–20% of the viewport height.
