Below this, the left sidebar occupies about 25–30% of the page width, running nearly full height under the header. The right main content column uses the remaining 70–75% width. Within the main content, reserve roughly the top 40% of its height as a dense information band split into a wider left text column (~65%) and a narrower right pricing/CTA column (~35%). The bottom ~60% is dominated by the product-image-viewer, anchored on the bottom-right and spanning about 60–70% of the main content width. Internal paddings are modest (around 10–16px), keeping everything compact and slightly crowded in a deliberate, catalog-like way.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain high information density, echoing early-2000s catalogs. Use smallish fonts and tight line spacing for navigation, specs, and secondary labels. Vertically, keep gaps between stacked elements slim (about 6–10px). Only around the product image do we allow more open white space, especially on its right and lower edges, so the image feels like it floats in the content area. Grouped panels like the pricing form and specs block have thin paddings (10–12px) and minimal margin between them, giving a sense of packed, utilitarian information rather than airy minimalism.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Encourage a Z-shaped visual flow. The eye starts at the logo in the top-left, moves across the glossy top nav to the top-right where the mini-cart indicator sits, then drops down into the left sidebar categories. From there it jumps right into the main content: first to the breadcrumb and bold product title in the upper-left of the main area, then across to the pricing/add-to-cart block in the upper-right of that same area. The gaze then flows downward through specs and pack options and finally comes to rest on the large, saturated product image at the bottom-right. This creates a clear progression from orientation (breadcrumb), to identification (title), to decision info (price, specs, options), to the visual confirmation (image).

layout.overall_description: The page appears as a fixed-width, early-2000s industrial catalog. A glossy header spans the full width at the top, with the logo on the left and a strong red navigation bar running horizontally just beneath it; at the far right of this bar, a compact mini-cart indicator shows the current cart count. Below, a tall purple-toned left sidebar lists tarp categories in a tight vertical stack, filling roughly a quarter of the page width. To the right, the main product detail column begins with a slim breadcrumb bar just under the header. Immediately below, the upper portion of the main column forms a dense information band: on the left, the product title, rating, shipping info, core specs, and pack options are stacked tightly; on the right, a boxed pricing-and-quantity form with an Add to Cart button and a small favorites control at its edge. This band occupies about the top two-fifths of the main column, with modest padding and minimal gaps. Beneath it, a large, colorful product image fills the lower-right area, reaching toward the right and bottom edges of the content frame. The open space around the image contrasts with the packed text above, reinforcing a diagonal flow from the top-left title area down to the bottom-right image, in line with an old-school corporate catalog aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay confined to the right main content column (it does not cover the global header or left sidebar). It is positioned absolutely within the main content area, spanning 100% of the main column’s width and height from just under the breadcrumb down to the bottom of the viewport. At its center, a small spinner or progress indicator is displayed against the slightly dimmed content. The overlay respects the fixed-width layout, aligning with the main column’s left and right edges, and leaves the top nav and category sidebar visible but visually de-emphasized while loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a full-cover overlay on the main content column; positioned absolute with high z-index, blocking interaction with all underlying components until loading completes. It scrolls with the page but is typically shown only at initial load or during data refreshes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the very top of the right main content column, directly below the global header and perfectly aligned with the main column’s left edge. It spans nearly the full width of the main content (about 95–100%), with a slim vertical footprint (roughly 24–32px tall). A light, slightly glossy bar or subtle bottom border separates it from the header above. The text is left-aligned, small, and tightly spaced, leaving only about 8–10px padding above and below. There is a small gap of about 6–8px before the product-header-summary begins underneath, maintaining the compact, linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that scrolls out of view with the rest of the content; it does not stick to the top. It anchors the top of the main content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product header summary is placed immediately below the breadcrumb, anchored to the left side of the right main column. It occupies roughly the left 65% of the main content width within the upper information band, leaving the right 35% for the pricing-and-quantity-form. The title is bold and slightly larger than the body text but still compact, typically spanning one to three lines. Beneath the title, a short descriptive sentence or tagline fits within the same 65% width, with very tight vertical spacing (around 4–6px between lines and 6–8px between the title and description). The block has modest padding (10–12px) only on its internal sides if placed within a light-tinted panel; externally, it sits about 6–8px below the breadcrumb and about 8–10px above the rating-summary-display, maintaining a dense stack on the left side of the top section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, left-anchored block within the upper content band; does not resize responsively beyond the fixed-width column but may allow text wrapping to multiple lines within its allotted width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites toggle is a compact control positioned in the upper-right quadrant of the main content’s top information band, visually associated with both the product header and the pricing area. It is aligned to the top-right corner of the pricing-and-quantity-form, typically appearing as a small star icon with a text label like “Add to Favorites,” right-justified. It sits just a few pixels (4–6px) below the horizontal line where the product title appears on the left, creating a subtle diagonal alignment from title to favorites to mini-cart (in the header). The control itself occupies a small footprint (e.g., 80–120px wide) and is padded minimally (about 6–8px), ensuring it doesn’t overpower the pricing box below but remains easy to spot when scanning the top-right area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, tied to the top edge of the pricing-and-quantity-form block. It scrolls with the page and does not float or stick independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-image-viewer dominates the lower portion of the right main content column, anchored firmly to the bottom-right. It begins just below the top information band (which includes header, ratings, specs, options, and pricing), leaving a slim gap of about 12–16px between the text band and the image area. Horizontally, the image viewer spans roughly 60–70% of the main content width, aligned with the right edge of the column so that its right side nearly touches the main content margin. Vertically, it consumes most of the remaining height (about 60% of the main column height), leaving more open white space on its bottom and right edges than elsewhere on the page, creating a visual resting zone. The left side of the image block may have a bit more whitespace (or overlap partly under the left text area) to reinforce a diagonal composition: dense text in the upper-left, expansive image in the lower-right. Any thumbnails or zoom controls are tucked along the left or bottom edges of the image panel, compact and tightly spaced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, placed at the bottom of the main column’s content stack. It grows vertically as needed but remains anchored to the right, maintaining its role as the visual endpoint of the page’s flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product specs panel is located in the left portion of the upper information band, directly beneath the rating and shipping row and still within the same ~65% width as the product-header-summary. It starts about 6–8px below the rating-summary-display and shipping-indicator, forming a tight vertical stack. The panel may be enclosed in a very light, slightly recessed box or separated by a subtle top border, and includes a compact bulleted list of key specs (material, thickness, size, etc.). The specs list uses small fonts and minimal vertical spacing between bullets (about 4–6px), allowing 5–8 bullet points to fit in a relatively short vertical space. The panel stretches toward the right until it nearly meets the internal boundary between the left text column and the right pricing column, but does not overlap. It fills most of the remaining left-column height within the top 40% information band, with the pack-options-selector anchored directly below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level element in the left text column, expanding vertically as needed. It scrolls with other content and does not collapse or float by default, though it could allow a simple show-more link within its boundaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rating summary display is a narrow horizontal strip placed immediately beneath the product-header-summary in the left text column. It begins about 6–8px below the descriptive text and spans roughly 50–60% of the left column width, leaving room on its right side for the shipping-indicator on the same horizontal line. Star icons and rating numbers are aligned to the left, with the overall height of the row kept small (around 24–28px tall). Vertical space above and below is minimal, so the rating feels like a subline of the title rather than its own isolated section. This tight integration contributes to the dense, catalog-like feel at the top-left of the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline-block-like row within the left column. It remains horizontally aligned with the shipping-indicator and moves with the page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping indicator shares the same horizontal row as the rating-summary-display, but is aligned to the right edge of the left text column. Within that row, ratings appear on the left and the shipping indicator (such as an icon and text like “Ships in 1–2 days”) appears on the right, creating a balanced line across the width of the 65% text column. Its width is flexible but typically occupies about 40–50% of the row, right-justified. The overall row sits approximately 6–8px beneath the product-header-summary, with minimal padding above and below, visually tying shipping information tightly to the title and ratings without additional separators.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned element sharing a horizontal line with the rating summary. It scrolls with the left text column and adjusts its width based on content but remains within that row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pack options selector is positioned directly below the product-specs-panel within the left text column, still confined to roughly 65% of the main content width. It starts about 8–10px beneath the bottom of the specs list, forming the last major element in the top-left information stack before the large image section. The control might be a compact dropdown, a small grid of radio buttons, or a simple list of buttons representing different pack sizes; in any case, the controls are tightly arranged, with limited horizontal padding (8–10px) and minimal vertical gaps (4–6px) between options. Its total height is modest—typically fitting within 60–100px—so that it stays within the top 40% band of the main content and finishes before the product-image-viewer begins below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level form control group within the left column, expanding slightly vertically if more pack options exist. It scrolls with the content and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing-and-quantity-form occupies the entire right slice of the main content’s upper information band, taking roughly 30–35% of the main content width from just below the breadcrumb down to just above the product-image-viewer. It forms a prominent, boxy panel aligned to the right edge of the main column, with a subtle beveled or glossy header strip in line with early-2000s styling. Inside, the price is displayed near the top, followed by quantity controls and an Add to Cart button stacked vertically, all packed with modest padding (10–12px) and minimal vertical gaps (6–8px). The favorites-toggle is visually docked at its top-right corner. The panel’s height extends to match most of the height of the left text column’s content (title, ratings, specs, options), so that together they form a cohesive rectangular band across the top ~40% of the main column before the layout transitions to the image area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-anchored form panel. It maintains its width and relative vertical position within the fixed-width layout and scrolls with the page. It does not float or become sticky, reflecting the more static patterns of early-2000s designs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mini-cart indicator lives in the global header, embedded within the glossy top navigation bar at the far right. Horizontally, it is aligned flush with the right edge of the overall page container (the same width as the main content and sidebar), and vertically it shares the same height as the nav bar (roughly 40–50px). It appears as a small icon plus item count or a compact pill-shaped element, slightly raised or glossy, with very tight internal padding (6–8px). There is little whitespace around it; it sits immediately adjacent to the last navigation tab to its left, reinforcing the dense, utility-focused top bar. It is always above the left sidebar and main content in the stacking order, making it easy to locate whenever the header is visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the header: it is part of the non-sticky top navigation area and scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down the page. It does not float over content or remain fixed to the viewport.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Allows users to review all items in their cart (standard products and custom tarps), adjust quantities, remove items, see current totals, and proceed to checkout.

core_features: Load the current cart and all line items (standard products and custom tarp configurations) using getCartSummary.
Display each line item with its name/summary, configuration details (for custom tarps), unit price, quantity, and line subtotal.
Allow inline editing of item quantities and persist changes via updateCartItemQuantity, refreshing cart totals on success.
Allow removal of individual items from the cart via removeCartItem, updating totals and handling an empty-cart state.
Display cart subtotal and estimated total before shipping and taxes from getCartSummary.
Block checkout initiation when the cart is empty and inform the user why checkout cannot continue.
Initiate a checkout session via beginCheckout and navigate to checkout_shipping.html with the returned checkout_session_id in the URL.
Handle and display error states for failed API calls or invalid user input without hardcoding any product or price data.
user_workflows:
View and review cart contents:
1. User navigates to cart.html (optionally with URL parameters such as ?highlightItem=...).
2. On page load, the page-init logic calls getCartSummary.
3. The response populates the cart line items list and the cart summary panel with subtotal and estimated total.
4. If the cart has no items, an empty-cart state is shown instead of the line items list.

Update the quantity of a cart item:
1. User locates a line item in the cart line items list.
2. User changes the quantity via an input field or increment/decrement controls.
3. On change (or after explicit confirmation), the page calls updateCartItemQuantity with the cart_item_id and new quantity.
4. While the request is in progress, the UI can disable the quantity control for that item.
5. On success, the specific line item’s quantity and line_subtotal are updated from updated_item, and the cart summary panel is updated using cart_summary.
6. On failure, an error is shown in the error banner component, and the quantity input is reverted to the last known valid value.

Remove a cart item:
1. User clicks a Remove control on a line item.
2. A confirmation step may appear (client-side) to avoid accidental removal.
3. On confirmation, the page calls removeCartItem with the item’s cart_item_id.
4. On success, the line item is removed from the list and the cart summary panel is updated using cart_summary.
5. If the result indicates total_quantity is zero, the empty-cart state is shown and the checkout button is disabled.
6. On failure, the line item remains visible and an error message is displayed.

Proceed to checkout from the cart:
1. User reviews their cart and confirms quantities and items.
2. User clicks the Proceed to Checkout button in the cart summary panel.
3. If the cart is empty (based on the current cart state from getCartSummary or the latest cart_summary), the action is blocked and a message is shown indicating that checkout requires at least one item.
4. If the cart has items, the page calls beginCheckout.
5. On success, the returned checkout_session_id and current_step are used to construct a navigation URL.
6. The user is redirected to checkout_shipping.html with the checkout_session_id passed via a URL parameter (for example, checkout_shipping.html?session_id=[checkout_session_id]).
7. On failure, the user remains on cart.html and an error message is displayed via the error banner.
interactions:
Page load and cart retrieval:
- Trigger: Browser loads cart.html, possibly with URL parameters such as highlightItem, returnUrl, or errorCode.
- Behavior: A page-init component calls getCartSummary with no parameters.
- The cart items list and cart summary panel are populated with the response.
- If a highlightItem parameter is present and matches a cart_item_id, the page focuses or scrolls to that item.

Quantity change interaction:
- Trigger: User updates a quantity control for a specific line item.
- Behavior: The new quantity is validated client-side (e.g., must be a positive integer, not empty). If invalid, the change is rejected and an inline validation message is shown.
- If valid, the component calls updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, quantity).
- Result: On success, only the affected line item and the cart summary are updated using data from the API response. On error, the prior quantity is restored and an error appears in the error banner.

Remove item interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks the Remove button/link for a line item.
- Behavior: Optionally show a confirmation prompt (purely client-side). On confirmation, call removeCartItem(cartItemId).
- Result: On success, the line item is removed from the list and the cart summary is updated. If total_quantity becomes zero, show the empty-cart state and disable checkout. On error, keep the item visible and show an error message.

Proceed to checkout interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks Proceed to Checkout in the cart summary panel.
- Behavior: Check the current cart state (from the latest getCartSummary or the last returned cart_summary) to ensure total_quantity is greater than zero.
- If the cart is non-empty, call beginCheckout().
- Result: On success, redirect to checkout_shipping.html with checkout_session_id as a URL parameter. On error, show an error message and keep the user on cart.html.

Navigate to product or configuration details:
- Trigger: User clicks an item name or a View details link.
- Behavior for standard products (item_type = 'standard_product'): navigate to product.html with the product.id passed as a URL parameter (e.g., product.html?id=[product.id]).
- Behavior for custom tarps (item_type = 'custom_tarp'): navigate to the custom tarp configuration page (e.g., custom_tarp_builder.html?config_id=[custom_tarp_config.id]) so the user can review or adjust their configuration (if supported by that page).

Handling URL-driven focus or messaging:
- If a URL parameter such as highlightItem is provided, the page scrolls to and highlights the matching cart item after getCartSummary resolves.
- If a URL parameter such as errorCode or message is provided, the error banner interprets it (client-side) and displays an appropriate high-level message without hardcoding any product data.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Cart page state is driven by API responses and URL parameters, not by localStorage.

URL parameters:
- No URL parameter is required to load the cart; cart.html without parameters always attempts to load the current open cart via getCartSummary.
- highlightItem (optional): If present and equal to a cart_item_id returned by getCartSummary, the corresponding line item is programmatically focused or highlighted after data load.
- returnUrl (optional): If present, this URL is used for a 'Continue shopping' link or button. When not present, 'Continue shopping' may rely on browser history or a default navigation path defined elsewhere.
- errorCode or message (optional): If present, these are read by the error banner component to display an appropriate message (for example, if the user was redirected back from a later checkout step). The mapping from errorCode to text is handled client-side.

Cart state:
- The primary cart state is the result of getCartSummary, including cart, items[], subtotal, and estimated_total_before_shipping_and_tax.
- After quantity updates or item removals, the authoritative cart summary is the cart_summary object returned by updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem. The page replaces the previous subtotal and total_quantity with these values, then requests getCartSummary again only when a full refresh is needed (e.g., after multiple operations or when re-entering the page).

Checkout initiation and navigation state:
- When Proceed to Checkout is clicked, beginCheckout is called without parameters.
- On a successful beginCheckout response, the checkout_session_id is injected into the checkout shipping URL as a query parameter (e.g., checkout_shipping.html?session_id=[checkout_session_id]). The current_step field can be checked client-side to ensure the step is 'shipping' before navigation.
- No cart state is stored in URL parameters; instead, the checkout session object in the backend (referenced by checkout_session_id) ties the browser’s subsequent steps to the current cart.

components.functionality: Initializes the Cart page by parsing URL parameters and loading the current cart contents and totals via getCartSummary. Manages the top-level loading state, error state, and triggers item highlighting if requested.

Displays the list of cart items returned by getCartSummary, including both standard products and custom tarps. For each item, shows name/summary, configuration details, unit price, quantity, and line subtotal, and exposes controls for changing quantity and removing the item.

Handles the quantity input for a single cart item, including client-side validation and invoking updateCartItemQuantity. Disables itself while an update is in progress and updates the item’s line_subtotal on success.
Provides a remove action for a single cart item and calls removeCartItem when confirmed. On success, it triggers removal of the item from the items list and updates the cart summary.

Displays aggregated cart information including subtotal and estimated total before shipping and taxes, based on getCartSummary and updates from updateCartItemQuantity/removeCartItem. Provides the Proceed to Checkout action and may show notes about when shipping and taxes are calculated.

Implements the logic for starting checkout from the cart. Validates that the cart is not empty, calls beginCheckout, and on success navigates to checkout_shipping.html with the checkout_session_id passed in the query string.

Shows an empty-cart message and a navigation option when there are no items in the cart (for example, after getCartSummary or after a removal that results in total_quantity = 0). Hides the items list and disables checkout-related actions.
Displays error messages resulting from failed API calls (getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, beginCheckout) or from URL parameters such as errorCode. Can be dismissed by the user.

Parses and reacts to URL parameters impacting the Cart page (e.g., highlightItem, returnUrl, errorCode). Coordinates with other components to highlight a given item, configure 'Continue shopping' navigation, or display error messages.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width two-column layout, likely based on an old-school table or float structure rather than a formal modern grid (approx. 960–1000px total width, left column ~25%, right column ~75%).
layout.layout_pattern: The cart page sits inside the same global shell as the mockup: full-width logo at the very top, then a bold horizontal navigation bar directly beneath it. Below that, the page splits into two columns: a persistent left sidebar for product/category navigation and a right main-content area for the cart itself. Within the main content, the cart follows a top-to-bottom, left-to-right reading order: error/status banner at the top (if present), then the cart title and line-item list, and finally a visually emphasized cart summary and checkout section anchored toward the bottom-right, echoing the mockup’s product-image emphasis.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The cart page sits inside the same global shell as the mockup: full-width logo at the very top, then a bold horizontal navigation bar directly beneath it. Below that, the page splits into two columns: a persistent left sidebar for product/category navigation and a right main-content area for the cart itself. Within the main content, the cart follows a top-to-bottom, left-to-right reading order: error/status banner at the top (if present), then the cart title and line-item list, and finally a visually emphasized cart summary and checkout section anchored toward the bottom-right, echoing the mockup’s product-image emphasis.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header + top navigation occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. Below, the left sidebar takes about 25–30% of the total page width and extends nearly full height with stacked navigation links. The main content area to the right occupies the remaining 70–75% width. Inside the main content, the cart-items list dominates the upper and central area, using about 60–65% of the vertical space, while the cart-summary panel plus checkout trigger occupy roughly the lower 35–40%, aligned to the right side to recreate a strong bottom-right focal block. Side paddings and gutters remain modest (around 10–16px) to keep the layout compact and dense.
layout_strategies.content_density: The cart follows the high-density, early-2000s catalog style: line items are presented in a tight table-like layout with small fonts, minimal vertical spacing between rows, and compact inline controls for quantity and remove. The summary panel is also information-dense, stacking subtotal, tax, shipping, and total with limited line spacing. Padding inside panels is functional rather than generous, with roughly 8–12px internal margins. The only relatively open space is around the right and bottom edges of the cart-summary panel, which visually mimics the open area around the product image in the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the logo at the top-left to the bold horizontal navigation bar. From there, the colored left sidebar pulls attention downward along the navigation categories. The user’s gaze then shifts right into the main content: first catching any error banner, then scanning down through the cart line items. Finally, attention settles on the larger, more visually solid cart-summary panel and its checkout button anchored in the bottom-right of the main content, creating a familiar Z-shaped path: top-left (logo), across the nav, down the left sidebar, then across and down through the cart list to the summary/checkout in the lower right.

layout.overall_description: The cart page appears within a fixed-width, early-2000s corporate shell: a full-width logo bar at the top, followed by a bold, glossy horizontal navigation strip. Below, a solid-colored left sidebar fills roughly a quarter of the width with tightly stacked category links, grounding the layout. To its right, the cart content lives in a white main panel that stretches across the remaining width. At the top of this panel, a narrow, full-width error banner may appear, followed by a compact cart heading and then a dense, table-like list of cart items running down from the top-left. Each row packs product name, options, quantity controls, and a remove link with minimal spacing. As the user scrolls or scans downward, their eye is drawn to a more substantial cart-summary box that sits in the lower-right of the main content area, with a subtle gradient and framed edges, containing totals and a wide, glossy checkout button along its bottom edge. This summary block and checkout action form the visual anchor of the page, occupying the role that the large product image held in the original mockup, and giving the layout a clear, utilitarian Z-flow from header to final action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component is non-visual and occupies no visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the main content’s DOM hierarchy, just after the global header and sidebar markup, initializing and wiring up the cart state before any visual cart components render. It does not affect spacing or alignment; the page’s left sidebar, main cart area, and all visible components render as if this controller were not there.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic-only component; no rendered box, no dimensions, no positioning. Executes before layout of visible cart components, but does not interact with scrolling or fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart items list is the dominant block within the right-hand main content area. It begins just below any error banner and page title, aligned flush to the left edge of the main content with a modest left padding of about 12–16px to match the rest of the site. It spans roughly 85–90% of the main content width, leaving a small right margin into which the cart-summary panel can visually tuck. Vertically, it occupies the upper and central portion of the main content: starting near the top and extending downward until roughly two-thirds of the main content height, or as long as needed if there are many items. The list itself is rendered as a dense, table-like stack of rows with a faint header bar at the top (for labels like Product, Options, Quantity, Price, Remove). Row heights are compact, with minimal vertical padding (around 4–6px per row) and narrow horizontal gaps between columns. Text is left-aligned; numeric values like price and quantity cluster toward the right side of each row. The overall block feels like a utilitarian spreadsheet embedded within the page, anchored firmly to the top-left of the main content, while leaving visual space in the bottom-right for the summary panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block element within the main content; scrolls with the page. In older-layout terms, it can be considered the main right-column content floated none or left, with the summary panel allowed to float to its right or sit beneath depending on height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity control appears inside each row of the cart-items list, positioned in the quantity column toward the right half of the table. It occupies a small horizontal segment, roughly the width of a short input field with tiny up/down controls or plus/minus buttons, giving a slightly skeuomorphic, form-control feel. Vertically, it is centered within the row’s height, with minimal surrounding whitespace to maintain high density. It sits immediately to the left of the line item’s price or total, and just to the left of the remove control, forming a compact cluster of interactive elements at the far right of each row. Its alignment is right-leaning but not flush to the table edge, leaving a few pixels of padding to keep it from touching the row border.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline-block or table-cell element within each row; its width is fixed or minimally flexible so that columns align vertically across rows. It scrolls with the cart list and does not detach or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove item control is the rightmost element within each cart row, positioned at the far end of the action cluster. It appears as a small text link or a narrow button (e.g., a red text link or an icon with label) aligned to the right edge of the row’s content area, with only a few pixels of padding from the right boundary of the table cell. Vertically, it is centered in the row, sitting directly in line with the quantity control to its immediate left. The control is compact and understated in size but clearly differentiated by color, providing a quick way to trim items without consuming much horizontal space. Across all rows, remove controls line up in a straight vertical column near the right edge of the cart-items list, reinforcing the dense, tabular character of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline or table-cell element; it does not affect row height or table width significantly. It scrolls naturally with the cart-items list and remains in place as items are added or removed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary panel is positioned in the lower-right region of the main content block, echoing the large product image placement from the mockup. Horizontally, it is right-aligned within the main content area, with about 12–16px padding from the right edge to maintain the page’s modest gutter. Its width is roughly 35–40% of the main content width, making it narrower than the cart-items list but visually substantial. Vertically, it starts around the point where the cart-items list begins to taper off, typically in the lower third of the viewport; where content is short, it clearly occupies the bottom-right of the visible page; when the list is long, it appears as the user scrolls down, still anchored to the right. The panel is rendered as a boxed section with a subtle gradient or glossy top strip and a fine border, containing stacked lines: subtotal, shipping estimate, tax, discounts, and final total. These lines are left-aligned labels with right-aligned amounts, arranged with tight vertical spacing (about 4–8px between rows). The bottom portion of the panel houses the checkout-trigger button, which spans most of the panel’s width, reinforcing this area as the final focal point of the cart.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block or right-floated element within the main content. In a classic float-based layout, it may float right while the items list occupies the remaining left area above or alongside; it scrolls with the page and is not fixed or sticky, but visually behaves like an anchored bottom-right block when the content is short.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout trigger is visually rendered as a prominent, glossy button inside the cart-summary panel, positioned at the very bottom of that panel. It spans approximately 80–90% of the panel’s internal width and is horizontally centered, leaving a narrow margin on either side. Vertically, it is separated from the totals lines above by a small gap (about 8–10px) to create a clear action zone. The button’s height is slightly larger than standard form controls, giving it more visual weight in an otherwise dense layout. It uses a strong, saturated color—mirroring or complementing the bold top navigation bar—to stand out against the panel’s light background. When the cart content is short, the checkout button sits close to the bottom-right edge of the overall page content, effectively replacing the product hero image as the final point of attention in the user’s scanning path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Child element of the cart-summary panel; it does not exist outside that panel in layout terms. It scrolls with the summary panel and has no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state display appears in place of the cart-items list and summary when the cart has no items. It is positioned within the main content area, starting near the top-left, just below any error banner and the cart page title. The block spans roughly 60–70% of the main content width, aligned flush to the left with the same 12–16px left padding as the items list, and has a modest height (around 25–35% of the viewport) containing a short message and one or two calls to action (e.g., links back to categories). The message is enclosed in a lightly bordered or shaded box that feels consistent with the site’s utilitarian aesthetic but leaves slightly more whitespace around the text than the dense line-item view. The space to the right and below remains mostly empty white canvas, echoing the open background around the product image in the original mockup, making the emptiness of the cart visually clear without resorting to large illustrations.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the cart-items list and summary panel when active. It is a standard block element within the main content and scrolls normally, though in typical cases it will fit entirely above the fold.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner is a slim, full-width bar at the very top of the main content area, directly beneath the global horizontal navigation and above the cart heading and items list or empty state. It spans the entire width of the main content column (the 70–75% right side of the page) with minimal vertical height, roughly the equivalent of one or two lines of text plus 4–6px padding above and below. The banner uses a strong, attention-grabbing background color, typically red or orange, with bold white or dark text, and may include a small icon on the left. It runs from the inner left padding of the main content to the inner right padding, maintaining the same gutter as the rest of the page. The tight vertical spacing keeps it from feeling like a hero stripe; instead, it functions like a compact status bar that shifts the rest of the cart content downward when visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element at the top of the main content; it is not fixed and scrolls offscreen as the user scrolls down. Its presence pushes all subsequent cart components downward, but it does not overlay them.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This URL state handler component is non-visual and does not render any visible UI. Conceptually, it lives alongside the cart-page-init near the top of the cart page’s component tree, watching the URL for parameters that affect the cart (e.g., applied coupons, return-from-checkout states) and updating underlying state before or alongside rendering the visual components. It does not impact the spatial arrangement, size, or position of any visible element on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered logic component with no box model; it has no scrolling, fixed, or absolute behavior and does not influence layout calculations directly.

name: Checkout - Shipping & Address
title: Checkout - Shipping & Address
description: Collects or confirms the shipping address for the active checkout session, lets the user select a shipping method, shows a cart summary, and advances the checkout to the payment & review step.

core_features: Load the current checkout shipping step state, including any existing shipping address and available shipping methods, using getCheckoutShippingStep.
Load and display a read-only cart summary for the active cart using getCartSummary so the user can verify items and subtotal.

Display an editable shipping address form that is pre-filled when getCheckoutShippingStep returns an existing ShippingAddress, and allow the user to create or update the address via setCheckoutShippingAddress.

Display a selectable list of available shipping methods from getCheckoutShippingStep, with the current selection highlighted based on selected_shipping_method_id, and allow the user to change the selection using selectCheckoutShippingMethod.
Validate that a shipping address has been saved and a shipping method has been selected before allowing the user to proceed.
Advance the checkout from the shipping step to the payment & review step using continueCheckoutToPayment, then navigate to checkout_payment.html on success.
Provide a way to navigate back to cart.html to review or edit cart contents, without modifying the checkout session on this page.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial load of Shipping & Address step
1) User arrives on checkout_shipping.html after starting checkout from the cart.
2) On page load, the client invokes getCheckoutShippingStep.
3) The page inspects the returned checkout_session, shipping_address, available_shipping_methods, and selected_shipping_method_id.
4) The shipping address form is pre-filled if shipping_address is present; otherwise the form is empty.
5) The list of shipping methods is rendered from available_shipping_methods and the pre-selected option (if any) is determined by selected_shipping_method_id.
6) In parallel, the client invokes getCartSummary to display current cart items, quantities, and estimated totals in a summary section.
7) The Continue to Payment control is enabled only when both a shipping address is present in the form state and a shipping method is selected.

Workflow: Enter a new shipping address
1) User focuses the shipping address form and fills in full name, street address, city, state, postal code, and country fields.
2) User submits the form (e.g., clicks a Save/Use This Address action within the shipping address form or Continue to Payment if address auto-save is supported).
3) The client gathers the current form field values and calls setCheckoutShippingAddress with fullName, streetAddress, streetAddress2, city, state, postalCode, and country.
4) If the response indicates success, the component updates its local state with the returned shipping_address and considers the address valid for subsequent steps.
5) If success is false or the call fails, the component surfaces an error state and keeps the user on this step with the form still editable.

Workflow: Edit an existing shipping address
1) On load, the form is pre-filled from shipping_address returned by getCheckoutShippingStep.
2) User modifies one or more fields (for example, changing the postal code or city).
3) User submits the form, triggering setCheckoutShippingAddress with the updated field values.
4) On success, the existing shipping_address associated with the CheckoutSession is updated and used for subsequent operations.
5) The page keeps the user on the shipping step but shows the updated information as the active address.

Workflow: Select or change a shipping method
1) User views the list of available_shipping_methods, each with a selectable control (for example, radio input) bound to the method id.
2) When the user selects a shipping option, the component triggers selectCheckoutShippingMethod with the chosen shippingMethodId.
3) If the response indicates success, the component updates local state to reflect the selected_shipping_method and ensures that selection persists within the page session.
4) If the call fails, the selection is reverted or flagged as invalid, and a message is displayed while keeping the user on the same step.
5) The Continue to Payment button remains disabled until a successful shipping method selection has been confirmed.

Workflow: Proceed to Payment & Review
1) User ensures the shipping address form is valid and a shipping method is selected (both conditions must be met according to the page state).
2) User clicks Continue to Payment.
3) The component performs final client-side checks that a shipping address has been saved (based on the latest successful setCheckoutShippingAddress response or existing shipping_address) and that a shipping method is associated with the checkout session (based on selected_shipping_method_id or the last successful selectCheckoutShippingMethod result).
4) If prerequisites are met, the component calls continueCheckoutToPayment.
5) On success, the returned checkout_session.current_step should be updated to 'payment'; the client then navigates to checkout_payment.html (optionally preserving or updating URL parameters as defined in the state logic).
6) If the call is not successful, an error is displayed, and the user remains on the shipping step with all entered data retained.

Workflow: Return to Cart from Shipping step
1) User decides to change cart contents and clicks Back to Cart.
2) The click handler navigates the browser to cart.html, optionally adding URL parameters (for example, a return context) but without calling any checkout interface from this page.
3) The existing CheckoutSession on the server side remains available; if the user later resumes checkout and returns to checkout_shipping.html, getCheckoutShippingStep will repopulate the previous state.

Workflow: Reload or revisit the Shipping page
1) User refreshes checkout_shipping.html or returns to it from another step (for example, via browser back from checkout_payment.html).
2) On load, the page again calls getCheckoutShippingStep and getCartSummary.
3) Any previously saved shipping_address and shipping_method_id are reloaded from the server and used to pre-fill the form and selected option.
4) User may adjust the address or shipping method, triggering setCheckoutShippingAddress or selectCheckoutShippingMethod as needed.
5) User can again continue to payment using the updated information.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Event: onLoad for checkout_shipping.html.
- Actions: Call getCheckoutShippingStep to retrieve checkout_session, shipping_address, available_shipping_methods, and selected_shipping_method_id. Call getCartSummary to retrieve cart, items, subtotal, and estimated_total_before_shipping_and_tax. Bind responses to relevant components (address form, shipping methods list, cart summary, step indicator).

Interaction: Shipping address field changes
- Event: onChange for each address input field (full name, street address, street address 2, city, state, postal code, country).
- Actions: Update local form state and clear any address-specific error messages. Optionally mark the address state as 'dirty' to prompt re-saving before continuing.

Interaction: Submit shipping address
- Event: onSubmit for the shipping-address-form component (e.g., when user clicks a Save/Use This Address button, or when Continue to Payment is clicked and unsaved changes are detected).
- Actions: Validate that required fields are non-empty on the client, then call setCheckoutShippingAddress with the form values. On success, update local shipping_address state; on failure, display error messages and prevent advancement to payment.

Interaction: Select shipping method
- Event: onChange for a selectable control (such as a radio button or list selector) associated with each ShippingMethod id.
- Actions: Immediately call selectCheckoutShippingMethod with the selected shippingMethodId. While awaiting response, optionally show a loading indicator and temporarily disable further method changes. On success, update local selected_shipping_method state and enable Continue to Payment if the address is also valid. On failure, revert the selection or keep previous selection, show an error, and keep Continue to Payment disabled if no valid selection exists.

Interaction: Click Continue to Payment
- Event: onClick for the primary button to proceed.
- Actions: Check local state to confirm a saved shipping_address and a selected_shipping_method are present. If the address form has unsaved changes, first trigger the onSubmit handler which calls setCheckoutShippingAddress; only proceed if that succeeds. Once prerequisites are met, call continueCheckoutToPayment. If successful, navigate to checkout_payment.html, optionally passing URL parameters representing the current step. If not successful, display a non-blocking or blocking error and remain on the page.

Interaction: Click Back to Cart
- Event: onClick for the Back to Cart control.
- Actions: Navigate to cart.html using location change, optionally preserving a return context parameter in the URL. No checkout interfaces are called in this interaction from this page.

Interaction: Error handling and retry
- Event: onError events triggered by failed calls to getCheckoutShippingStep, setCheckoutShippingAddress, selectCheckoutShippingMethod, continueCheckoutToPayment, or getCartSummary.
- Actions: Display contextual error messaging in the relevant components (for example, inline near address fields, near shipping method selection, or as a global message). Provide a Retry action that re-invokes the relevant interface call without modifying other page state.

Interaction: Refresh or direct re-entry
- Event: Browser refresh or navigation back to checkout_shipping.html.
- Actions: onLoad logic is re-run (getCheckoutShippingStep and getCartSummary). Previously saved server-side state repopulates the page. Any client-only state (such as unsaved address edits) is lost and must be re-entered by the user.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on server-managed CheckoutSession state exposed through getCheckoutShippingStep, but URL parameters are used to maintain navigation context and optional flags:

- Base URL: checkout_shipping.html with no required parameters; the active checkout session is resolved on the server side and returned by getCheckoutShippingStep.

- Optional parameter: checkout_shipping.html?from=cart
  - Indicates that the user navigated here directly from cart.html.
  - Used client-side only to determine which page to return to when the user clicks Back to Cart (e.g., always cart.html, but can be extended to handle alternative return flows without extra interfaces).
  - This parameter does not alter how getCheckoutShippingStep or any other interface behaves; it only affects navigation decisions.

- Optional parameter: checkout_shipping.html?step=shipping
  - Can be used for consistency with other checkout steps, making it explicit in the URL which step is being displayed.
  - On load, the page may verify that checkout_session.current_step from getCheckoutShippingStep is compatible with the 'shipping' value; if not, the client can decide to redirect to the appropriate step (for example, to checkout_payment.html) using standard navigation, without calling additional interfaces.

- Optional parameter: checkout_shipping.html?error=...
  - May be appended when returning from another page or after handling an error server-side, to show a contextual message on this page.
  - On load, the client reads this parameter and displays a corresponding non-hardcoded error message (e.g., mapped to generic client-side error types). It does not alter any interface calls.

No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent browser storage. All authoritative information about cart, shipping address, and shipping method is obtained from the assigned interfaces on each page load. URL parameters are read-only hints that influence which page to navigate to or what client-side messages to show, but they never override data returned by the interfaces.

components.functionality: On page load, retrieves all data necessary for the Shipping & Address step by calling getCheckoutShippingStep and getCartSummary, and distributes that data to child components.

Displays the current checkout step and related steps (e.g., Shipping, Payment, Complete) based on checkout_session.current_step, and ensures the user understands they are on the Shipping & Address step.

Provides input fields for full name, street address, optional street address line 2, city, state, postal code, and country. Pre-fills values from shipping_address when available and allows the user to create or update the address by calling setCheckoutShippingAddress.

Displays a list of all available shipping methods from available_shipping_methods, marks the method whose id matches selected_shipping_method_id as selected, and allows the user to change the selection by calling selectCheckoutShippingMethod with the chosen shippingMethodId.

Shows a read-only summary of the cart, including each line item, quantities, and monetary totals before shipping and tax, using data from getCartSummary to help the user confirm they are shipping the correct items.

Provides Back to Cart and Continue to Payment actions. Back to Cart navigates to cart.html using URL navigation only. Continue to Payment checks that a shipping address and shipping method are present, optionally triggers address save via setCheckoutShippingAddress if there are unsaved form changes, and then calls continueCheckoutToPayment. On success, it navigates to checkout_payment.html.

Aggregates and displays error or status messages related to loading the shipping step, saving the address, selecting a shipping method, continuing to payment, or loading cart summary, based on the outcomes of the assigned interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width two-column layout, likely based on an old-school table or float structure rather than a formal modern grid (approx. 960–1000px total width, left column ~25%, right column ~75%).
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global logo and horizontal navigation bar, the page splits into a strong left column and a larger right column. The left column is used as a dense, persistent checkout/cart summary sidebar, mirroring the category navigation role in the mockup. The right column is the main checkout flow: at the very top of this column sits the checkout-step indicator, followed (when present) by error messages. Beneath that, the shipping address form occupies the upper part of the right column, the shipping method selector sits directly below it, and the navigation controls (Back / Continue) anchor the bottom-right of the content area. All components stack vertically within their column, in a linear top-to-bottom flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global logo and horizontal navigation bar, the page splits into a strong left column and a larger right column. The left column is used as a dense, persistent checkout/cart summary sidebar, mirroring the category navigation role in the mockup. The right column is the main checkout flow: at the very top of this column sits the checkout-step indicator, followed (when present) by error messages. Beneath that, the shipping address form occupies the upper part of the right column, the shipping method selector sits directly below it, and the navigation controls (Back / Continue) anchor the bottom-right of the content area. All components stack vertically within their column, in a linear top-to-bottom flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed-width page (about 960–1000px) is centered on the viewport. The header (logo + nav, not included in the component list) takes the top 15–20% of viewport height. Below the header, the main content area is split: the left checkout-cart-summary-sidebar occupies roughly 25–30% of the page width, stretching almost the full remaining height; the right main checkout column takes the remaining 70–75% width. Within the right column, the top ~15% of column height is reserved for the step indicator and any error messages; the shipping-address-form occupies roughly the next 35–40%; the shipping-method-selector uses about 20–25%; and the checkout-navigation-controls sit in the bottom 10–15% of the column, with a modest margin from the page bottom. Margins and paddings are compact (~10–16px) to maintain a dense, catalog-like feeling.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is tightly packed, with small fonts, tight line spacing, and modest padding around blocks. The cart summary sidebar uses a stacked list of items with minimal vertical spacing. The shipping address form arranges labels and inputs in compact rows (and where possible, two-column field groupings) to keep the form visually dense. The shipping method list uses snug spacing between options. White space is modest except around the outer edges of the main columns; there is no generous breathing room, reinforcing a busy, utilitarian interface. Only the lower-right area around the navigation buttons has slightly more open space, echoing the mockup’s emphasis around the product image zone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the top-left logo and the bold horizontal navigation bar, down into the prominent colored cart summary sidebar on the left. From there, attention naturally shifts rightward to the top of the main checkout column where the step indicator sits. The eye then tracks downward through the shipping address form and the shipping method options, finally landing on the bottom-right navigation controls. This creates a Z-like and then L-shaped reading path: across the header, down the left sidebar, across to the right column’s top, and down to the bottom-right controls, closely echoing the product-page flow from logo to nav to categories to product details to product image.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a fixed-width, early-2000s-style catalog layout centered in the browser window. At the very top, a full-width brand logo area and a bold, glossy horizontal navigation bar span the page, occupying the top slice of the viewport. Directly beneath this, the main content area is divided into two columns. On the left, a tall, colored sidebar panel holds the cart summary, running almost from just below the header down to the page’s visual bottom, with tightly stacked line items and totals. On the right, a wider main column begins with the checkout step indicator aligned to the top, styled like a small tabbed strip. Immediately below it, any error messages appear as a narrow but high-contrast bar. The shipping address form fills the upper-middle portion of this right column with dense rows of labels and input fields, some arranged in side-by-side pairs to conserve space. Below that, the shipping method selector presents a grouped list of options inside a bordered box, again with tight vertical spacing. Anchoring the entire composition, the bottom-right of the right column holds the navigation controls—Back to Cart on the left, Continue to Payment on the right—within a slightly more open band of space, making them the final focal point after scanning down the page. A subtle loading overlay can cover the main columns when needed, but in the default state, the page reads as a compact, utilitarian checkout screen with a strong left sidebar and a form-focused right content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is an overlay layer that sits above the entire main content area (both the left cart summary sidebar and the right checkout column) but below the global header. It is positioned starting just under the horizontal navigation bar and extends down to the bottom edge of the fixed-width page content. Visually, it can appear as a semi-transparent white or light-gray wash covering both columns, slightly dimming the underlying content while keeping the basic structure visible. Centered horizontally within the main content width and placed in the upper third of the overlay (roughly aligned over the top portion of the right column) is a compact loading indicator—such as a small spinner and text—occupying no more than 20–25% of the page width. The overlay respects the underlying fixed-width layout, not stretching beyond it, reinforcing the table-like, centered page frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay anchored to the main content container (not the full browser window), covering both sidebar and main column when active; otherwise hidden. It scrolls together with the page content if vertical scrolling is present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout step indicator is positioned at the very top of the right main column, directly beneath the horizontal site navigation and horizontally aligned so its left edge lines up with the left edge of the right column (just to the right of the cart summary sidebar). It spans nearly the full width of the right column—about 70–75% of the total page width—and has a short vertical height, roughly equivalent to a strip of tabs (around 40–60px tall). It appears as a glossy, horizontally segmented bar with each step represented as a tab, with the current step (Shipping & Address) in a stronger, more saturated color while the other steps are dimmer. A narrow gap (about 8–12px) below it separates it from any error messages or from the top of the shipping address form when no errors are present. The indicator does not extend over the left sidebar; it is visually confined to the right content area, serving as the header band for the main checkout content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element at the top of the right column; it moves upward and out of view if the user scrolls, consistent with a non-sticky, table-based layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping address form sits in the upper-middle portion of the right column, immediately beneath the step indicator and any error message panel. Its left edge aligns cleanly with the left edge of the right column, and its right edge aligns with the right column’s inner margin, giving it the full usable width of this column (approximately 70–75% of the page width). Vertically, it occupies about 35–40% of the right column’s height, forming a substantial block of dense fields. The form title (e.g., 'Shipping Address') is a modestly bold line at the top of this block, separated from the step indicator above by a compact gap of roughly 8–12px. Inside the form, labels and input fields are stacked in tight rows with minimal vertical spacing. Where possible, related fields (such as First Name / Last Name, City / State / ZIP) are arranged in two-column sub-rows so that each row spans the full form width, reinforcing the compact, catalog-like feel. Left padding is modest (about 10–12px), and the form may be visually enclosed with a thin border or subtle gradient header bar, echoing old-school sectional boxes. The bottom of the form ends with a small vertical gap (around 12–16px) before the shipping method selector begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right column, expanding vertically as fields dictate. If the viewport height is small, the form content scrolls naturally with the page; it is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping method selector is placed directly below the shipping address form in the right column, aligned to the same left and right edges so it also uses the full width of the main column. It occupies a shorter vertical slice than the address form—roughly 20–25% of the right column’s height, depending on the number of shipping options. The component appears as a bordered or softly shaded box with a small header line (e.g., 'Shipping Method') at its top, followed by a vertically stacked list of shipping options, each represented by a radio button, method name, cost, and possibly estimated delivery. Each option row is tightly spaced with small font and minimal padding between lines, maintaining high information density. The box has modest internal padding (about 10–14px) on all sides and a narrow gap (about 10–12px) beneath it before the navigation controls begin. Taken together with the address form above, it creates a continuous vertical flow of form-driven content down the right side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component that grows vertically if additional shipping options are present; it pushes the navigation controls downward accordingly, consistent with a simple stacked layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary sidebar occupies the entire left column beneath the global header, starting just below the horizontal navigation bar and continuing down towards the bottom of the main content area. It takes up approximately 25–30% of the total page width and is visually separated from the right column by a vertical divider or a noticeable difference in background color. The sidebar is styled as a solid, slightly saturated panel (e.g., a purple or blue tone) with a subtle gradient at the top header area (e.g., 'Order Summary'). Inside, items are listed in a compact vertical stack with very tight line spacing and minimal padding between entries, reflecting the dense catalog navigation feel from the mockup. Subtotals, shipping estimates (once selected), and total cost appear towards the bottom of this panel, possibly in a small bordered subsection. The sidebar has modest internal padding (around 10–14px) from its edges and maintains a consistent left margin to the browser edge and a narrow right margin before the right column begins. It extends almost the full height of the content area; if there are many line items, the sidebar can scroll with the page, but each entry remains compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, left-aligned column element that scrolls normally with the page. It is not sticky; when the user scrolls down, the entire sidebar moves up, consistent with a fixed-width, table-like layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation controls sit at the bottom of the right column, directly below the shipping method selector and above any page bottom padding or footer. They form a horizontal band across the full width of the right column, with a height of roughly 60–80px including vertical padding. Within this band, the primary 'Continue to Payment' button is right-aligned, positioned near the bottom-right corner of the right column, echoing the mockup’s emphasis on the bottom-right product image area. The secondary 'Back to Cart' control is placed to its left on the same baseline, with a small horizontal gap (about 8–12px) between them. The band has slightly more open space above and below the buttons compared to other components, making this area feel subtly more breathable and drawing the eye as the final focus of the checkout step. The top edge of the band is separated from the shipping method selector by a narrow margin (around 10–16px), and the band ends with a similar modest gap before the page’s bottom boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored at the bottom of the content stack in the right column. It moves with page scroll and does not remain fixed to the viewport; its position is determined by the height of the content above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error message panel for the shipping step is located in the right column, between the checkout-step indicator and the shipping address form. It spans the full width of the right column, matching the horizontal alignment of those components, and has a relatively short height, typically one to three lines of text. When present, it occupies a narrow horizontal band (roughly 40–60px tall) with a high-contrast background color (e.g., red or yellow) and a subtle border or icon, making it clearly visible but still compact. Vertical padding inside the panel is modest (around 6–10px), and the text is small but legible, with dense line spacing. A small gap (about 6–10px) separates it from the step indicator above and from the top of the shipping address form below. When no errors are present, this band collapses completely, allowing the shipping address form to shift upward so that it sits directly beneath the step indicator, maintaining the same linear flow without leaving unused vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component that appears and disappears in place; it does not overlay other content. Its presence simply pushes the shipping address form downward within the right column’s stacked flow.

name: Checkout - Payment & Review
title: Checkout - Payment & Review
description: Page where the user reviews all order details (items, shipping address, shipping method, and costs), enters payment information, and navigates back to shipping or cart, or forward to order completion.

core_features: Load the current checkout session, including shipping address, selected shipping method, order items, and price breakdown via getCheckoutPaymentReview.
Display a complete order item list with quantities, unit prices, and line subtotals for both standard products and custom tarp configurations.
Show the selected shipping address and shipping method with an option to navigate back to the shipping step to edit them.
Present a cost summary including order subtotal, shipping cost, estimated tax, and overall order total.
Provide a placeholder payment information form area (e.g., card details and billing address selection) without persisting data to any interface in this scope.
Offer navigation controls to go back to the cart page or to proceed to an order completion page (beyond current task scope).
Use getCartSummary to support a header mini-cart or consistency check between cart totals and the current checkout summary.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arrive from shipping step and review order
1. User is redirected from checkout_shipping.html to checkout_payment.html (optionally with a cs parameter representing the checkout session ID).
2. On page load, the page calls getCheckoutPaymentReview to retrieve checkout_session, shipping_address, shipping_method, order_items, and price breakdown.
3. In parallel or immediately after, the page calls getCartSummary to populate the header mini-cart and validate cart totals.
4. The user reviews the step indicator to confirm they are on the Payment & Review step.
5. The user scans the order item list, confirming quantities, product details, and line subtotals.
6. The user reviews the shipping address and shipping method summaries.
7. The user reviews the order cost summary (subtotal, shipping, estimated tax, and total).
8. The user enters payment information into the placeholder payment form (local UI state only at this stage).
9. The user clicks the button to proceed, triggering navigation toward the order completion page (e.g., checkout_complete.html) while preserving any cs URL parameter if present.

Workflow 2: Edit shipping details from the payment step
1. User is on checkout_payment.html and sees the shipping address and method summary.
2. User clicks the 'Edit Shipping Details' link or button within the shipping summary section.
3. The click handler navigates the browser to checkout_shipping.html, appending the cs parameter from the current URL if present and an additional returnTo=payment parameter to indicate the intended return step.
4. On the shipping page, the user modifies the shipping address and/or selects a different shipping method (handled by that page).
5. The shipping page, upon continuation, navigates back to checkout_payment.html, preserving cs and returnTo information as needed.
6. On load, checkout_payment.html calls getCheckoutPaymentReview again, now reflecting updated shipping details and costs.

Workflow 3: Return to cart from payment & review
1. User is on checkout_payment.html reviewing the order.
2. User decides to adjust items in the cart instead of continuing.
3. User clicks the 'Back to Cart' control in the navigation section or header.
4. The click handler navigates to cart.html, optionally including from=checkout_payment and propagating cs if present.
5. On the cart page, the user adjusts quantities or removes items (handled there).
6. When the user proceeds from cart to shipping and then to payment again, checkout_payment.html reloads and calls getCheckoutPaymentReview, reflecting the updated cart contents.
interactions: On initial page load, trigger getCheckoutPaymentReview to populate checkout session details, order items, shipping information, and cost breakdown, and trigger getCartSummary to populate the mini-cart.

When the user clicks any step in the step indicator that corresponds to an earlier step (e.g., Shipping or Cart), perform a client-side navigation to the appropriate page (checkout_shipping.html or cart.html), carrying over the cs parameter if present.
When the user clicks 'Edit Shipping Details' within the shipping summary, navigate to checkout_shipping.html with cs (if present) and returnTo=payment in the query string.
When the user clicks 'Back to Cart', navigate to cart.html with from=checkout_payment and cs (if present) in the query string.
When the user edits payment fields in the placeholder payment form, update only local form state; no interface calls are made for payment processing in this scope.

On payment form submission (e.g., clicking 'Place Order' or 'Continue'), perform client-side validation. If validation passes, navigate to the next step (e.g., checkout_complete.html) while preserving cs in the URL if it exists; actual order creation and payment processing are out of scope for this page.

If getCheckoutPaymentReview returns a checkout_session whose current_step is not 'payment', use client-side logic to redirect the user to the appropriate step page (e.g., redirect to checkout_shipping.html if current_step is 'shipping'), including cs parameter when available.

If getCartSummary indicates a cart status that prevents checkout (e.g., not open), display an appropriate message and optionally redirect to cart.html; the exact behavior can be determined by global checkout rules.
page_functionality.state_logic: This page does not require any parameters for its data interfaces, but uses URL parameters to manage navigation state and continuity between steps. The primary URL parameter is `cs`, representing a checkout session identifier, which is passed along in navigations to checkout_shipping.html, cart.html, and a future checkout_complete.html to keep the user tied to the same checkout session; the backend remains free to also rely on cookies or server session to resolve the active CheckoutSession when getCheckoutPaymentReview is invoked. An optional `returnTo` parameter can be set (e.g., `returnTo=payment`) when navigating from this page to checkout_shipping.html so that the shipping page knows which step to return to after updating shipping details. An optional `from` parameter can be used when navigating back to cart.html (e.g., `from=checkout_payment`) to allow the cart page to adjust its UI or post-navigation behavior. On load, the page reads any existing `cs` from the URL and stores it in local runtime state for reuse in link generation only (not for interface calls). After calling getCheckoutPaymentReview, the page checks the returned `checkout_session.current_step`; if it differs from 'payment', the page uses this state to perform a client-side redirect to the step indicated by `current_step`, again propagating `cs` as a URL parameter if present. The step indicator component uses `checkout_session.current_step` as the authoritative source for what step should be marked active, falling back to a `step` URL parameter only for display overrides if such a parameter is defined by broader application routing, but it does not alter interface calls.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by calling getCheckoutPaymentReview to retrieve the active checkout session, shipping address, shipping method, order items, and cost breakdown, and calls getCartSummary to populate the mini-cart and validate cart status. Stores the results in a shared page state that other components consume.

Displays the multi-step checkout sequence (Cart, Shipping, Payment & Review, Complete) and highlights the current step based on checkout_session.current_step. Allows navigation to earlier steps (Cart and Shipping) through click interactions that change the browser location.

Renders the detailed list of order items returned by getCheckoutPaymentReview, including standard products and custom tarp configurations. For each item, shows product name or configuration summary, quantity, unit price, and line_subtotal. Does not modify quantities on this page; it is read-only, with changes directed back to the cart.

Displays the shipping address used for this checkout, using the ShippingAddress associated with the CheckoutSession. Provides an 'Edit Shipping Details' control that sends the user back to the shipping step to adjust address and shipping method.

Shows the selected shipping method (name, optional description, and delivery estimate derived from the ShippingMethod associated with the CheckoutSession). Indicates whether shipping cost has been applied in the totals and references the cost shown in the order cost summary component.

Displays the monetary breakdown returned by getCheckoutPaymentReview: order_subtotal, shipping_cost, estimated_tax, and order_total. This component is read-only and is recalculated only when getCheckoutPaymentReview is called again (e.g., after editing shipping on another step).

Provides a placeholder form section where the user can enter payment-related information such as a payment method selection and billing address options. The form maintains local field state and performs client-side validation, but does not call any interfaces to process or store payment data in this scope. On successful validation, it delegates to the navigation-controls component to move the user to the order completion step.

Shows a compact summary of the current cart in the page header, including total quantity and subtotal, using the data from getCartSummary. Ensures visual consistency between the active checkout session and the underlying cart; if discrepancies are detected according to global business logic, it can prompt the user to review their cart.

Provides the main navigation actions for this page: going back to the cart, editing shipping details, and proceeding toward order completion. The component reads the current URL parameters (e.g., cs) and constructs navigation URLs to cart.html, checkout_shipping.html, and a future checkout_complete.html, preserving cs and adding contextual parameters such as from and returnTo.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width two-column layout, likely based on an old-school table or float structure rather than a formal modern grid (approx. 960–1000px total width, left column ~25%, right column ~75%).
layout.layout_pattern: Use the classic header + left sidebar + main content pattern. The global header (logo, primary navigation, and mini cart) spans the full fixed-width page at the top.
