onGetAboutContentSuccess: Invoked when getAboutContent returns successfully. Updates internal state with the full response object and calls a helper to apply the current `section` value (e.g., scroll/focus).
onGetAboutContentError: Handles failure states from getAboutContent (e.g., showing a generic error message section and preventing child components from rendering content-dependent sections).

onSectionChange: Triggered when the `section` query parameter changes, either from in-page navigation clicks or browser history navigation. Updates the active section state and scrolls/focuses the corresponding component section, without recalling getAboutContent.
onPopState: Listens for browser back/forward events, re-parses the `section` query parameter from the URL, and delegates to onSectionChange.

components.functionality: Provides in-page navigation entries mapped to the logical sections of the About content (overview, key points, certifications). It keeps the active item synchronized with the `section` URL parameter and triggers section changes when items are clicked.
data_binding: getAboutContent.response.key_points (used only to decide whether to show or hide the navigation item for the Key Points section)
getAboutContent.response.certifications (used only to decide whether to show or hide the navigation item for the Certifications section)
event_handlers: onClickOverview: Sets the `section` query parameter to `overview` using the History API (e.g., pushState) and notifies the about-page-controller to update active section state and scroll/focus.
onClickKeyPoints: If key_points content exists, sets `section=key_points` in the URL and notifies the controller to update active section state and scroll/focus.
onClickCertifications: If certifications content exists, sets `section=certifications` in the URL and notifies the controller to update active section state and scroll/focus.

components.functionality: Displays the main About information, including the dynamic headline and rich-text body describing the company background, experience, and specialization in industrial tarps and protective covers.
event_handlers: onRender: Renders the headline and body_html once getAboutContent data is available. If the active section is `overview`, ensures this section is scrolled into view or focused as appropriate.
onActiveSectionUpdated: When notified by the page controller that the active section has changed, checks if it should highlight itself or scroll into view when `activeSection` is `overview`.

components.functionality: Renders a list of key points returned by getAboutContent, summarizing the company’s strengths, capabilities, or differentiators in industrial tarps and protective covers. If no key_points are present, the component remains hidden or non-rendered.
event_handlers: onRender: Checks if `key_points` is present and has items. If so, renders a list of items; if not, does not render the section.
onActiveSectionUpdated: When the active section changes, if `activeSection` is `key_points` and the section is rendered, scrolls or focuses this section.

components.functionality: Displays a list of certifications returned by getAboutContent, supporting user reassurance about product quality, compliance, or standards. If no certifications are provided, the section is omitted.
event_handlers: onRender: Checks if `certifications` is present and has items. If so, renders them as a structured list; otherwise, hides the section.
onActiveSectionUpdated: When the active section changes, if `activeSection` is `certifications` and the section exists, scrolls or focuses this section.

The left column remains a strong, full-height navigation stack for product categories. The right column is devoted to the About content. All content is left-aligned and flows vertically with minimal breaks, echoing the dense catalog feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the classic early-2000s catalog layout: global header and horizontal main nav at the very top (outside these components), then a two-column body controlled by the page controller. The left column remains a strong, full-height navigation stack for product categories. The right column is devoted to the About content. Within the right column, the About page flows top-to-bottom: first the main About heading and introductory copy, then a tight in-page navigation bar, followed by two list-based sections (key points first, certifications second). All content is left-aligned and flows vertically with minimal breaks, echoing the dense catalog feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the header, the about-page-controller centers a fixed-width content area around 960–1000px. Sections are visually separated by subtle rules, header bands, or background tints rather than large whitespace. layout.overall_description: Below the global logo and red horizontal navigation bar, a fixed-width content area sits centered on the page. On the left, a tall purple-toned sidebar runs from just under the header almost to the footer, filled with tightly stacked product category links. To the right, on a white background, the About content forms a vertical column. At the top of this column, a bold “About” heading and a short block of text introduce the company, aligned to the left with narrow side paddings. Directly beneath this introduction, a glossy, skeuomorphic in-page nav bar with tab-like links lets users jump to different parts of the About content without leaving the page. Scrolling down, the first major block is a list of key points about the company’s industrial tarp expertise, appearing as a dense, catalog-style bullet list anchored to the left side. Below that, separated by a thin rule or subtle header band, the certifications list appears in a similar compact format, slightly more open on the right and bottom to echo the original mockup’s product-image breathing room. The overall impression is utilitarian and information-heavy, with a strong structural backbone from the left sidebar and a clear top-to-bottom reading path in the main About column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-controller spans the full width of the content area beneath the global header and horizontal main navigation, centering a fixed-width layout of approximately 960–1000px. Inside, it enforces a two-column structure: a left sidebar occupying about 25–30% of the page width, and a right main column occupying the remaining 70–75%. The left column extends from just below the top navigation down toward the bottom of the viewport, maintaining a solid colored background (e.g., purple gradient) and housing dense site-wide product category links (outside this component list). The right column begins flush with the top of the left sidebar content and runs vertically down the page, with modest left and right padding (10–16px) and a white background. All About-related components (in-page nav, main content, key points, certifications) are stacked in the right column in a linear top-to-bottom flow. The page-controller is responsible for keeping the entire body content fixed in width and centered on the screen, with vertical scrolling affecting both columns together.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a fixed-width, centered container for the two-column layout; scrolls with the page, with no sticky or floating behavior. It controls how child components snap into the left-versus-right columns and maintains their relative proportions regardless of viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-in-page-nav is positioned in the right main column, directly beneath the introductory content from the About heading area and above the detailed list sections. It sits flush left within the right column, spanning roughly 70–80% of the right column’s width, leaving a modest margin on the right to preserve some white space and avoid a full-bleed feel. Vertically, it occupies a shallow band—about 40–50px high—with tight padding so the bar feels compact and slightly compressed, matching the site’s dense aesthetic. The nav appears as a horizontal strip of closely spaced text links or glossy tabs (e.g., “Overview,” “Key Points,” “Certifications”), aligned to the left with small gaps between each item. There is only a small vertical gap (about 8–10px) separating this bar from the introductory text above and from the content sections below, so the user perceives it as part of the same tight content block rather than a large transitional element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in the normal document flow within the right column; it scrolls off-screen with the rest of the content. Clicking items triggers in-page navigation (scrolling or jumping) to anchors in the main content, key-points, or certifications sections without changing the core layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-main-content-section sits at the top of the right column, directly under the global header and aligned horizontally with the top of the left sidebar navigation. It spans nearly the full width of the right column, minus modest side paddings (10–16px) that keep text away from the edges. At its top is the main About heading, styled as a bold, slightly larger line of text flush-left, with only a small margin above and below to maintain a compact header band feel. Immediately beneath the heading, one or two short paragraphs present the dynamic company background and industrial tarp expertise copy. This block occupies roughly the upper 25–35% of the visible right-column area when the page first loads. The text is arranged in a single column, with tight line spacing and small vertical gaps between paragraphs to echo catalog-style specifications. The bottom of this section sits just above the about-in-page-nav bar, separated by a narrow vertical gap (around 8px), so the heading and copy visually lead straight into the in-page navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document order at the top of the right column and expands vertically as needed to fit the dynamic content. It does not overlap other elements and will push subsequent components (in-page nav, lists) downward as content grows, maintaining the two-column structure defined by the page controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-key-points-section is the first major content block beneath the about-in-page-nav in the right column. It begins just a small distance (8–12px) below the nav bar, anchored flush-left, and spans about 70–80% of the right column’s width, leaving a small margin to the right similar to the nav bar. At the top of this section, a compact subheading such as “Key Strengths” or “Why Our Tarps” can appear as a bold line, followed immediately by a dense vertical list of bullet points summarizing the company’s capabilities and differentiators. Bullets are tightly stacked, with minimal spacing between items to create a catalog-like, information-heavy feel. The section may occupy roughly the middle 30–40% of the right column’s vertical space on a typical screen, depending on the number of bullet points. There is only a thin horizontal rule or a small gap separating the bottom of this section from the certifications section below, keeping them visually close and part of a continuous narrative down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides in the standard document flow of the right column, expanding vertically with the number of bullet points. It will push the certifications section further down as content grows, while maintaining its left alignment and relative width within the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-certifications-section appears directly beneath the key-points section, aligned to the same left margin in the right column. A subtle divider—such as a thin horizontal line or a slightly darker section title bar—marks the transition between key points and certifications, but the gap between them remains small (approximately 10–14px) to keep the dense, continuous flow. This section spans roughly the same width as the key-points section (about 70–80% of the right column width), but its content is allowed to sit with a touch more breathing room on the right and bottom, echoing the more open area that would normally be reserved for a product image. Certifications are listed as short bullet points or a compact table-like list, vertically stacked with tight spacing, emphasizing legitimacy and standards compliance. Visually, this section occupies the lower portion of the right column, likely the bottom 20–30% of the main content area on an average screen, forming the final focal zone in the user’s top-to-bottom reading path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits at the bottom of the primary About content stack in the right column and expands vertically based on the number of certifications. It scrolls normally with the page and does not exhibit sticky behavior, functioning as the natural end of the main content area before any global footer.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page where users can view dynamic contact details (phone, email, address, business hours) and submit inquiries via a contact form backed by getContactInfo and submitContactForm.

core_features: Retrieve and display current contact details (phone number, email address, physical address, business hours, and typical response time) using getContactInfo.
Provide a contact form that collects name, email, subject, and message and submits the data using submitContactForm.
Show real-time submission status and confirmation details (including ticket ID and confirmation message) based on the response from submitContactForm.
Support URL-parameter-based pre-filling of contact form fields (e.g., subject, topic hints, email) to streamline inquiries coming from other pages.
Allow deep linking to specific states of the Contact page (e.g., focus on the form, highlight a specific inquiry type, or display a confirmation banner after redirect) using URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Viewing contact information
1. User navigates to contact.html (e.g., via the footer link).
2. On page load, the page controller component calls getContactInfo with no parameters.
3. The response object is passed to the contact-info-panel component.
4. The contact-info-panel displays the phone_number, email_address, physical_address, business_hours, and typical_response_time returned by getContactInfo.
5. User may click the phone number or email address to initiate a call or open an email client using those dynamic values.

Workflow: Submitting a new general inquiry
1. User navigates to contact.html without special URL parameters (or with defaults).
2. The contact-page-controller parses URL parameters (if present) to prefill form fields (e.g., subject) but does not set any hardcoded values.
3. User fills in the contact form fields: name, email, subject, and message.
4. On form submission, the contact-form component performs client-side validation (e.g., required fields, email format) without using any backend other than submitContactForm.
5. If validation passes, contact-form calls submitContactForm with the current field values.
6. While waiting for the response, the form is set to a “submitting” state (e.g., submit button disabled, status component shows a pending state).
7. When submitContactForm returns, the contact-form-status component receives success, ticket_id, and confirmation_message from the response.
8. If success is true, contact-form-status displays the confirmation_message along with the ticket_id; the form may be cleared or left populated according to the functional spec.
9. If success is false, contact-form-status displays an error state and allows the user to retry.

Workflow: Contact form pre-filled from another page (e.g., product issue)
1. User clicks a "Contact" link from another page that includes URL parameters pointing to contact.html (for example, contact.html?subject=Question%20about%20product&topic=product_question&focus=form).
2. On load, contact-page-controller reads URL parameters such as subject, topic, email, or focus.
3. The controller passes these parsed values as initial state to the contact-form component.
4. The contact-form component pre-fills the subject field using the value from the subject URL parameter and may also pre-select an internal topic field (if implemented) based solely on URL parameters (no additional interfaces).
5. If focus=form is present, the page scrolls to or focuses the contact-form component so the user can start typing immediately.
6. The rest of the submission follows the standard submission workflow using submitContactForm.

Workflow: Showing confirmation after redirect using ticket ID in URL
1. User submits the contact form and submitContactForm returns a successful response with success set to true, a ticket_id, and a confirmation_message.
2. After acknowledging the successful submission, the page may update the URL (for example, appending or replacing the query string with ticket_id=<returned_ticket_id>&status=success) without reloading the page, or it may perform a full redirect to contact.html with those parameters.
3. On a subsequent load or reload of contact.html, contact-page-controller reads ticket_id and status from the URL parameters.
4. If status indicates success and a ticket_id is present, contact-form-status displays a confirmation message using the ticket_id in a read-only manner, without re-calling submitContactForm.
5. The contact-form component may render a cleared form ready for a new submission, while the status component continues to show the confirmation banner.

Workflow: Handling submission errors or network failures
1. User completes the contact form and triggers submission.
2. contact-form calls submitContactForm with the provided name, email, subject, and message.
3. If the interface returns success as false, contact-form-status shows an error state using information available in the confirmation_message field (if the backend uses it to convey error details) and prompts the user to adjust the form and retry.
4. If the call to submitContactForm fails at the network level (e.g., timeout), the contact-form component sets an internal error state and passes a generic error description to contact-form-status for display.
5. The user can correct any issues and re-submit, triggering submitContactForm again.
interactions: On page load, the page controller initializes state, reads URL parameters (such as subject, topic, email, focus, ticket_id, and status), and triggers getContactInfo to retrieve dynamic contact details.
User clicks the phone number displayed in the contact-info-panel, triggering an onClickPhone handler that uses the dynamic phone_number value to initiate a call via a tel: link.

User clicks the email address displayed in the contact-info-panel, triggering an onClickEmail handler that uses the dynamic email_address value in a mailto: link, optionally including a subject from URL parameters or current form subject state.
User focuses each input in the contact-form (name, email, subject, message) and types their information; each keystroke updates the component’s internal state bound to the form field values.

User clicks the submit button on the contact-form, which triggers onSubmitForm; this runs client-side validation and, if valid, calls submitContactForm with the current name, email, subject, and message state.

While the form is submitting, the submit button interaction is temporarily disabled and an onSubmissionInProgress state is propagated to contact-form-status to display a loading or pending message (without hardcoded timing).

When submitContactForm resolves, an onSubmissionResult handler processes the success flag, ticket_id, and confirmation_message, updating the contact-form-status component to show success or error feedback.
If the user navigates to contact.html with a URL parameter focus=form, a onPageReady handler scrolls or focuses the form container so keyboard input can start immediately in the first required field.

If the user modifies query-related fields (for example, subject) that were initially populated from URL parameters, those changes are kept in local component state; the URL is not required to update but may be updated by an onStateToUrlSync handler if desired for sharable links.

If the URL includes ticket_id and status, an onUrlStateDetected handler in the contact-page-controller passes these values to contact-form-status so it can display a confirmation banner without invoking submitContactForm again.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Contact page relies solely on URL parameters and in-memory page/component state (no localStorage) to manage its state. The base URL is contact.html. On load, the page controller parses the query string and interprets supported parameters as follows: 
- subject: Used to pre-fill the subject field in the contact-form. If present, the initial subject field value is set to this value; subsequent user edits do not automatically change the URL.
- topic: An optional hint used by the contact-form to set an internal topic selection (e.g., product_question, order_support, technical_question). The page does not call any additional interfaces based on topic; it only affects internal form defaults and labels.
- email: If present, used to pre-fill the email field in the contact-form for known or returning users arriving from other pages or flows.
- focus: If the value is form, onPageReady causes the viewport to scroll to and/or focus the contact-form, allowing immediate interaction.
- ticket_id: If present and accompanied by status, the page assumes this indicates a previously successful submission. This value is passed directly to contact-form-status for display; it is treated as read-only metadata and is not used to re-call submitContactForm.
- status: Used with ticket_id to determine if a confirmation state should be shown (e.g., status=success). The page does not infer any additional backend state from this parameter; it simply controls which banner the contact-form-status shows.
The results from getContactInfo are stored in page-level state and bound to the contact-info-panel so that any re-renders always rely on the latest retrieved values, without hardcoding them. Each submission through submitContactForm updates transient state (e.g., isSubmitting, lastSubmissionResult) inside the contact-form and contact-form-status components. No persistent storage beyond the current page lifecycle is used; if the user leaves or reloads the page without ticket_id and status in the URL, the confirmation state is lost by design.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and shared state for the Contact page. It calls getContactInfo on load, interprets URL parameters, and passes derived state down to child components (contact-info-panel, contact-form, and contact-form-status). It also manages optional URL updates after submission.
data_binding: URLParameters: subject, topic, email, focus, ticket_id, status
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse URL parameters, invoke getContactInfo, and initialize page-level state.
onContactInfoLoaded: Store getContactInfo results in contactInfo state and propagate them to contact-info-panel.
onUrlStateDetected: Detect ticket_id and status from URL and pass them to contact-form-status.
onSubmissionSuccess: Optionally update the URL with ticket_id and status without reloading, so the success state is shareable or restorable.
onFocusInstruction: If focus=form is present, scroll to or focus the contact-form component once both layout and initial data are ready.

components.functionality: Displays the dynamic contact information returned by getContactInfo, including phone number, email address, business hours, physical address, and typical response time. Provides click interactions for phone and email using only the dynamic values.
event_handlers: onClickPhone: Use the bound phone_number value to initiate a call via a tel: link in the user’s environment.
onClickEmail: Use the bound email_address value to open the default mail client via a mailto: link, optionally including a subject derived from current form state or URL parameters.
onContactInfoUpdated: Re-render the displayed details whenever the getContactInfo response changes (e.g., if the page re-fetches).

components.functionality: Collects user input for name, email, subject, and message, optionally uses URL parameters for initial values, and submits the inquiry using submitContactForm. Manages validation, submitting state, and propagates submission results to the status component.
data_binding: LocalState: name, email, subject, message, topic, isSubmitting, clientValidationErrors
event_handlers: onFieldChangeName: Update local name state on user input.
onFieldChangeEmail: Update local email state on user input.
onFieldChangeSubject: Update local subject state on user input.
onFieldChangeMessage: Update local message state on user input.
onTopicChange: Update local topic state if a topic selector is implemented, based solely on UI logic and URL hints.

onSubmitForm: Prevent default form submission, run client-side validation for required fields and email format, and, if valid, set isSubmitting to true and call submitContactForm with current name, email, subject, and message.

onSubmitResponse: When submitContactForm resolves, set isSubmitting to false, store success, ticket_id, and confirmation_message in local submission result state, and notify contact-form-status via an event or shared state.
onSubmitError: Handle network or unexpected errors by setting an error entry in clientValidationErrors or a separate error state and informing contact-form-status.
onInitializeFromUrl: On mount, read initial values passed down from contact-page-controller (parsed from URL) and populate name/email/subject/topic fields accordingly, without hardcoded defaults.

components.functionality: Displays real-time feedback about the contact form state, including submitting, success, and error states. Uses data from submitContactForm responses or from URL parameters (ticket_id and status) to show confirmation messages without re-calling the backend.
event_handlers: onSubmittingStateChange: When isSubmitting becomes true, display a loading or pending message so the user knows the form is being processed.
onSubmissionResult: When submitContactForm returns, read success, ticket_id, and confirmation_message and display an appropriate success or error banner.

onUrlConfirmationDetected: On initial load, if ticket_id and a success-like status are present in the URL, show a confirmation banner using ticket_id and a generic or URL-driven message, without calling submitContactForm.
onDismissStatus: Allow the user to dismiss the status banner, updating local visibility state while keeping any ticket_id information intact for potential reuse.

components.functionality: Manages focus and scroll behavior of the Contact page using URL parameters and page events. It ensures that when focus=form is specified, the viewport scrolls to the contact form and that, after certain operations (like a successful submission), focus is moved to the appropriate status or form element.
data_binding: PageStructure: references to contact-form and contact-form-status components
event_handlers: onPageReady: After initial rendering, check focus URL parameter and, if focus=form, scroll to and focus the first input of the contact-form.
onSubmissionSuccess: When contact-form reports a successful submission, move focus to the contact-form-status banner so assistive technologies announce the confirmation.
onStatusDismissed: If the status banner is dismissed, optionally return focus to the first field in the contact-form to facilitate a new submission.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width two-column layout, likely based on an old-school table or float structure rather than a formal modern grid (approx. 960–1000px total width, left column ~25%, right column ~75%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the classic early-2000s catalog structure: a full-width header with logo and bold horizontal navigation at the top, then a two-column body where the left column is a tall, continuous category/navigation sidebar and the right column holds the page-specific contact content. Inside the right column, the contact content is arranged top-to-bottom: page title and short intro, then a compact contact-info panel, followed by a slim status banner, and finally a large, vertically stacked contact form that visually occupies the role of the product photo from the mockup. This maintains the strong left-to-right, top-to-bottom diagonal emphasis.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the classic early-2000s catalog structure: a full-width header with logo and bold horizontal navigation at the top, then a two-column body where the left column is a tall, continuous category/navigation sidebar and the right column holds the page-specific contact content. Inside the right column, the contact content is arranged top-to-bottom: page title and short intro, then a compact contact-info panel, followed by a slim status banner, and finally a large, vertically stacked contact form that visually occupies the role of the product photo from the mockup. This maintains the strong left-to-right, top-to-bottom diagonal emphasis.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height and spans 100% width. Below, the content area is a fixed-width container (~960–1000px) centered on the page. The left sidebar takes about 25–30% of this width as a full-height navigation block, while the right column takes the remaining 70–75% for contact content. Within the right column, the upper 35–40% is allocated to the title, introductory copy, and contact-info panel, plus the slim status banner. The lower 60–65% is dominated by the contact form, which stretches toward the bottom-right corner, mirroring the product-image dominance on the reference page. Padding around inner blocks stays modest (10–16px).
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is dense and utilitarian. The left sidebar packs navigation links closely with small line height and minimal vertical spacing. In the right column, headings are slightly larger but still compact, with tight spacing between the title, intro text, contact-info bullet points, and the status banner. The contact form fields are vertically close, with little extra spacing between rows and modest padding inside input fields, evoking a table-like form layout. Margins between components are small, so the page feels information-heavy; the only more open visual area is the lower-right side of the form block, which has some breathing room at the bottom and right edges.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the logo at the top-left, then is pulled across to the bold horizontal navigation bar. From there, the strong colored left sidebar draws attention downward through the category links. The user then shifts right into the page title and intro copy at the top of the main content. The eye moves down-left through the contact-info panel, then along to the narrow status banner, and finally settles on the large contact form that fills the lower-right quadrant of the main content. This preserves a Z-shaped visual flow: top-left (logo), across the top nav, down the left sidebar, then across and down through the contact content to the form in the bottom-right.

layout.overall_description: Below a full-width header with a left-aligned logo and a bold, glossy horizontal navigation bar, the page is split into two columns inside a fixed-width, center-aligned container. The left column is a tall, purple-toned sidebar stacked with tightly spaced navigation links for product and catalog categories, running almost the full height of the page. To the right, a white main content panel presents the Contact page. At the top of this right panel, a compact 'Contact Us' heading and one-line intro sit flush to the left, with modest padding from the panel edges. Directly beneath, a contact-info panel lists phone, email, address, and hours in a tight, almost table-like block aligned to the left half of the column. Just under this block and spanning the main content width, a low-height status banner appears as a colored strip for success/error messages. Below that, filling the remainder of the right column and reaching towards the bottom-right corner, a dense, multi-field contact form dominates the view, with labels and inputs stacked vertically in a slightly old-school, form-like arrangement. The left navigation pulls the eye down, and then the form mass on the lower-right anchors attention, giving the page the same catalog-era, slightly skeuomorphic, information-heavy feel as the original product mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-controller owns the entire right-column content area under the global header, from just beneath the horizontal navigation bar down to the bottom of the content container and from the right edge of the left sidebar to the right page margin. It is rendered as a white or very light panel that occupies roughly 70–75% of the total page width and the full available height of the main content area. Inside this controller, at the very top-left of the panel, it places the 'Contact Us' page title and a short descriptive sentence, each on its own line with tight vertical spacing and about 12–16px padding from the top and left edges. Immediately below the intro line, aligned to the left and confined to the upper 35–40% of this right column, it positions the contact-info-panel. Beneath the contact-info-panel, spanning almost the entire width of the right column with 10–12px side padding, it slots the contact-form-status as a low-height horizontal strip. Below that, taking up the remaining 60–65% of the vertical space and allowed to scroll if needed, it lays out the contact-form so that it extends toward the bottom-right corner, with its right edge visually aligning with the right edge of the page controller panel. The internal spacing between these elements is minimal—around 8–10px vertical gaps—reinforcing the compact, dense feeling of the early-2000s catalog layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the document flow as the structural container for the Contact page’s right-column content; it scrolls with the page and does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel sits inside the contact-page-controller near the top of the right column, immediately under the page title and intro text. It is left-aligned and occupies roughly 60–65% of the available width of the right column, starting about 12–16px from the left inner edge and leaving a relatively lighter, more open area on its right side. Vertically, it fits within the top 25–30% of the right column’s height, appearing as a compact block of text: a small section heading like 'Contact Details' followed by dynamic lines or bullet-like rows for phone, email, address, and business hours. Line spacing is tight (small leading), and vertical gaps between items are around 4–6px, giving the impression of a dense, catalog-like data panel. There is modest internal padding (8–12px) around this block so that it feels like a distinct card or table-like area, but the margins between it and the elements above and below remain narrow, in keeping with the overall compact layout. Its content begins in the top-left of the panel and does not center; the right side of the panel ends roughly halfway to two-thirds across the column, allowing the lower portion of the form below to visually extend further toward the bottom-right corner, preserving the diagonal flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, anchored to the upper-left of the right column under the heading; it does not scroll independently or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form is the dominant block in the lower portion of the right column. It begins directly below the contact-form-status banner, with a small vertical gap of about 8–10px, and stretches downward to occupy roughly the bottom 60–65% of the contact-page-controller’s height. Horizontally, the form spans about 90–95% of the right column’s width, with consistent 10–12px padding from the left and right inner edges of the column so that its rightmost fields and buttons visually line up near the bottom-right corner of the content area. The form fields are stacked vertically in one or two tight columns: for example, name, email, phone fields arranged in a grid-like upper section, followed by a wide message textarea that spans almost the full width. Labels are left-aligned with a relatively small font, sitting immediately above or to the left of their corresponding inputs, with minimal vertical spacing (4–6px) between label and field. Between consecutive rows of fields, gaps remain compact (8–10px), giving the form a dense, table-like feel typical of older corporate sites. The submit button row appears at the bottom-right of the form area, with the primary button aligned to the right edge, helping to emphasize the lower-right corner and echo the role of a large product image in the original mockup. Any help text or validation messages appear just below or beside their respective fields, in small type, without adding much extra vertical space, preserving the overall tight layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page content; it scrolls with the page. Internally, the form may scroll only if it exceeds viewport height, but it generally behaves as a normal block-level element extending towards the bottom-right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form-status component is positioned inside the right column just below the contact-info-panel and above the contact-form. It appears as a thin horizontal banner, about 30–40px tall, spanning nearly the full width of the contact-page-controller with 10–12px side padding. The banner is aligned flush under the contact-info-panel with a small vertical gap (6–8px), creating a compressed stack of elements. When active, it uses a solid or gradient background color (e.g., green for success, red or amber for errors) reminiscent of early-2000s glossy alert strips, with the message text left-aligned and vertically centered within the band. When inactive, the space may collapse or reduce to a very minimal placeholder height so that it does not introduce excessive white space, maintaining the layout’s high information density. The status banner’s horizontal orientation connects the more compact contact-info block above with the broader, heavier form block below, acting as a thin visual divider without generous padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the content flow, expanding or collapsing height based on message presence; it does not overlay other content or stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-focus-manager is a non-visual behavioral layer logically associated with the contact-form and contact-form-status components rather than a visible block on the page. Conceptually, it wraps or hooks into the form area that occupies the lower portion of the right column and the slim status banner immediately above it. Its influence is felt in how focus moves: after submission, it may programmatically shift keyboard focus up to the contact-form-status banner at the top of the form region, then back down into the first errored field or the first input in the form. This preserves the perception of a continuous, compact block where feedback and form fields are tightly linked, without introducing any extra visible spacing or elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered and non-spatial; it does not occupy layout space. It behaves as an invisible controller tied to the DOM order of the contact-form and contact-form-status, affecting focus and keyboard navigation rather than physical positioning.

name: Shipping & Returns
title: Shipping & Returns
description: Page that loads and displays dynamic shipping information (including standard ground delivery estimates) and return/exchange policies for all tarps and protective covers.

core_features: Fetch combined shipping and returns content using getShippingAndReturnsContent on initial page load.
Display a shipping overview block using the returned shipping_overview_html content.
List all available shipping methods from the shipping_methods array, including each method’s name, description, and delivery estimate range.
Automatically identify and emphasize the shipping method with code = "standard_ground" so users can easily see typical standard ground transit times.
Display the returns and exchanges policy using the returned returns_policy_html content.
Support URL-parameter-driven deep linking to the shipping or returns sections (e.g., section state) and optionally to a specific shipping method (e.g., method=standard_ground).
Provide in-page navigation controls (e.g., tabs or links) to switch between the shipping content and returns content while keeping the URL in sync for sharable links.
user_workflows: View general shipping information: 1) User clicks the 'Shipping & Returns' link in the global footer (navigates to shipping_returns.html). 2) Page initializes and calls getShippingAndReturnsContent. 3) While loading, a loading state is shown. 4) On success, the shipping_overview_html is rendered at the top of the page. 5) The shipping methods list is rendered below, showing all available shipping methods from the shipping_methods array, including any estimated delivery ranges returned by the interface.

Check standard ground shipping timeframes: 1) User opens shipping_returns.html or follows a direct link that may include method=standard_ground. 2) Page calls getShippingAndReturnsContent and receives shipping_methods. 3) The component scans shipping_methods for the one with code = "standard_ground". 4) If found, that method is visually emphasized and, if method=standard_ground is present in the URL, scrolled into view. 5) User reads the method’s dynamically returned name, description, and delivery_estimate_min_days / delivery_estimate_max_days values to understand typical transit times.

Review the returns and exchange policy: 1) User navigates to shipping_returns.html with section=returns in the URL (or clicks an in-page 'Returns' navigation control). 2) Page calls getShippingAndReturnsContent (if not already loaded). 3) The current section state is set to 'returns' based on the URL parameter. 4) The view scrolls to (or focuses on) the returns policy section and visually indicates that the returns section is active. 5) The returns_policy_html content is rendered so the user can read the full return and exchange rules for tarps and protective covers.

Switch between shipping and returns content: 1) While on shipping_returns.html, the user clicks an in-page control to switch sections (e.g., 'Shipping' / 'Returns'). 2) The click handler updates the section parameter in the URL via the History API (e.g., ?section=shipping or ?section=returns) without a full page reload. 3) The page state updates to reflect the active section, ensuring the corresponding block (shipping or returns) is scrolled into view and visually highlighted. 4) The user can copy the current URL to share a direct link to the currently active section.

Navigate to a specific shipping method: 1) User is on shipping_returns.html viewing the shipping methods list. 2) The user clicks a control associated with a specific shipping method (e.g., a link or button in the list). 3) The handler updates the method parameter in the URL (e.g., ?section=shipping&method=<shipping_method.code>) and scrolls that method’s item into view. 4) If the user reloads or shares the URL, the page re-applies the method parameter to re-focus the same shipping method on load.
interactions: Footer navigation click: When the user clicks the 'Shipping & Returns' link in the global footer, the browser navigates to shipping_returns.html with optional query parameters (e.g., shipping_returns.html?section=shipping). No API is called from the footer itself; the new page handles data fetching on load.

Initial data load: On DOMContentLoaded or equivalent mounting event for the page, a page-level controller component triggers getShippingAndReturnsContent with no parameters. Success updates in-memory state with shipping_overview_html, shipping_methods, and returns_policy_html; failure sets an error state that can be displayed to the user.

Section toggle (shipping vs. returns): The user clicks an in-page control to view either the shipping or returns section. The click handler: (1) prevents default if using links for in-page navigation, (2) updates the URL’s section parameter (e.g., section=shipping or section=returns) via pushState/replaceState, and (3) updates local view state so only the corresponding section is active and optionally scrolls that section into view.

Shipping method focus: In the shipping methods list, each method item has an interactive element (e.g., a link) that, when clicked: (1) updates the URL’s method parameter to the clicked shipping method’s code, (2) marks that method as focused in component state, and (3) ensures the item is scrolled into view. If the focused method is standard_ground, the component also ensures any special emphasis styling is applied.

Back/forward navigation: When the user uses the browser’s back or forward buttons, a popstate event is handled by the page controller to re-read section and method from the current URL. The active section and focused shipping method are updated accordingly without re-calling getShippingAndReturnsContent, using the already-loaded data.

Error and retry: If getShippingAndReturnsContent fails (e.g., network error), the page shows an error message and a 'Retry' control. When the user activates the retry control, the same interface call is invoked again, and the loading state is re-applied until data is successfully retrieved.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s functional state is driven solely by URL parameters and in-memory view state derived from getShippingAndReturnsContent; no localStorage or other client persistence is used. The base URL is shipping_returns.html. Optional URL parameters include: (1) section: determines which main section is considered active, expected values like 'shipping' or 'returns'. If section is omitted or has an unexpected value, the default active section is 'shipping'. When section='shipping', the page ensures the shipping overview and shipping methods list are visible and, if necessary, scrolled into view. When section='returns', the returns policy area is scrolled into view and visually indicated as active. (2) method: used only when section is 'shipping' (or when section is omitted but interpreted as 'shipping'). This parameter is matched against the shipping_methods[i].code values returned by getShippingAndReturnsContent (e.g., 'standard_ground', 'expedited', 'overnight'). If a matching method is found, the corresponding item in the shipping methods list is flagged as focused and scrolled into view. If no matching method is found, no method-specific focus is applied. On initial load, the page parses the current URL’s query string to compute initial state: activeSection (from section) and focusedMethodCode (from method). After that, when the user interacts with section toggles or per-method controls, the code keeps the URL synchronized with the current state using history.pushState or history.replaceState. A popstate handler re-applies state from the URL whenever the user uses browser navigation. Data from getShippingAndReturnsContent (shipping_overview_html, shipping_methods, returns_policy_html) is not hard-coded and is always taken directly from the interface response.

components.functionality: Orchestrates initial data fetching, interprets URL parameters into view state, and passes shipping and returns content to child components. Manages loading, success, and error states and handles browser navigation events.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Called on DOMContentLoaded/mount; parses URL parameters (section, method) and invokes getShippingAndReturnsContent with no arguments. Sets internal state { shippingOverviewHtml, shippingMethods, returnsPolicyHtml, activeSection, focusedMethodCode, isLoading, error } based on the response and URL.

onPopState: Triggered when the browser back/forward buttons are used. Re-reads section and method from the current URL, updates activeSection and focusedMethodCode in state, and notifies child components to update their views accordingly without re-calling getShippingAndReturnsContent.

onRetryClick: Invoked when the user clicks a retry control in case of a previous load error. Re-invokes getShippingAndReturnsContent, resetting isLoading and error before the call, and updates state on success or failure.

onSectionChange: Receives a target section value from a child navigation component. Updates the URL’s section parameter using the History API and updates activeSection in state. Optionally also normalizes or clears the method parameter if the section is changed to 'returns'.

onMethodFocusChange: Receives a shipping method code from the shipping-methods-list component. Updates the method parameter in the URL and sets focusedMethodCode in state, so the focused method remains consistent with the URL.

components.functionality: Provides in-page controls (e.g., two navigation items) that let users switch between the 'Shipping' and 'Returns' sections. Reflects which section is currently active and updates the page controller when the user switches sections.
data_binding: URL.section (parsed into activeSection by the page controller)
getShippingAndReturnsContent.shipping_overview_html (used indirectly to decide if shipping content is available)
getShippingAndReturnsContent.returns_policy_html (used indirectly to decide if returns content is available)
event_handlers: onShippingClick: When the user selects the Shipping section control, calls a callback from the page controller (onSectionChange) with value 'shipping'. The controller then updates URL parameters and state; the navigation component re-renders, indicating the Shipping section as active.

onReturnsClick: When the user selects the Returns section control, calls the page controller’s onSectionChange with value 'returns'. The controller updates URL parameters and state; the navigation component re-renders, indicating the Returns section as active.

components.functionality: Displays the general shipping overview content using the shipping_overview_html HTML string returned by getShippingAndReturnsContent. Optionally indicates the default shipping method based on the shipping_methods data (e.g., showing a note or emphasis derived from the method where is_default = true). This section is visible and emphasized when the active section is 'shipping'.
event_handlers: onRender: Injects the shipping_overview_html into the DOM, ensuring it is only rendered once the data is successfully loaded. If shipping_methods is available, attaches any internal references (e.g., a mention of the default method) using the properties of the method where is_default is true, without hardcoding any specific method values.

onActiveSectionChange: Listens for changes to activeSection from the page controller. When activeSection becomes 'shipping', ensures this section is visible/expanded and can scroll into view if the URL’s section parameter indicates it should be focused.

components.functionality: Renders a list of all shipping methods returned in shipping_methods, showing each method’s name, description, and delivery estimate range using the dynamic data. Identifies the method with code = 'standard_ground' so users can easily locate standard ground details and uses focusedMethodCode to emphasize any method referenced by the URL.
data_binding: URL.method (via focusedMethodCode from the page controller)
event_handlers: onItemRender: For each item in shipping_methods, displays method.name, method.description, and the delivery_estimate_min_days and delivery_estimate_max_days values (if present) as the dynamic delivery estimate range. If method.code equals 'standard_ground', marks that item as the standard ground method (e.g., special label or emphasis) without hardcoding any other aspects of that method.

onItemClick: When the user clicks on a shipping method item (e.g., to focus or highlight it), invokes the page controller’s onMethodFocusChange with the clicked method’s code. This updates the URL parameter (method=<code>) and focusedMethodCode state, and the component re-renders so the focused item is visually distinguished and scrolled into view.

onFocusedMethodChange: When focusedMethodCode from the page controller changes (e.g., via URL changes or popstate), the component locates the method whose code matches focusedMethodCode and applies a focused style and scroll-into-view behavior for that item.

onActiveSectionChange: If activeSection is updated to something other than 'shipping', the component can hide or de-emphasize itself; when activeSection becomes 'shipping', the component ensures the list is visible and, if focusedMethodCode is set, scrolls the matching method into view.

components.functionality: Displays the returns and exchange policy for tarps and protective covers using the returns_policy_html HTML string returned by getShippingAndReturnsContent. Acts as the primary content area when the active section is 'returns'.
event_handlers: onRender: Injects returns_policy_html into the DOM only after it is loaded successfully. Ensures the section is part of the document structure so it can be targeted by scroll or focus when the active section changes.

onActiveSectionChange: When activeSection changes to 'returns', ensures this section becomes visible and scrolls into view if section=returns is present in the URL. When activeSection changes away from 'returns', the component may collapse or hide itself from immediate view while remaining in the DOM for direct links.

components.functionality: Handles display of errors that occur while calling getShippingAndReturnsContent and provides a user control to retry the request.
data_binding: getShippingAndReturnsContent (error state from the page controller)
event_handlers: onErrorStateChange: When the page controller sets an error state as a result of a failed getShippingAndReturnsContent call, this component renders an appropriate error message and a retry control. When the error state is cleared (after a successful retry), the component hides itself.

onRetryClick: When the user activates the retry control, invokes the page controller’s onRetryClick handler, which re-calls getShippingAndReturnsContent. During the retry, the component can update its display to indicate loading is in progress.

components.functionality: Displays a loading state while getShippingAndReturnsContent is in progress so users understand that shipping and returns information is being retrieved.
data_binding: getShippingAndReturnsContent (loading state from the page controller)
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange: Subscribes to isLoading from the page controller. When isLoading is true, shows a loading indicator instead of the main content sections. When isLoading becomes false and there is no error, hides the loading indicator so the main content components can be displayed.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width two-column layout, likely based on an old-school table or float structure rather than a formal modern grid (approx. 960–1000px total width, left column ~25%, right column ~75%).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the classic header + left sidebar + main content pattern from the mockup. Global elements (logo, top navigation bar, left category sidebar) sit outside these components. All listed components live in the right main content column. Within this column, content flows top-to-bottom in a linear sequence: first the page controller defines the interactive area, then the Shipping & Returns section navigation appears at the top of the main content. Below that, the Shipping Overview text section introduces policies, followed by the more detailed Shipping Methods data list, and finally the Returns Policy section. Error and loading states sit on top of or within this same stack, temporarily replacing or overlaying the normal content while maintaining the overall column structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the classic header + left sidebar + main content pattern from the mockup. Global elements (logo, top navigation bar, left category sidebar) sit outside these components. All listed components live in the right main content column. Within this column, content flows top-to-bottom in a linear sequence: first the page controller defines the interactive area, then the Shipping & Returns section navigation appears at the top of the main content. Below that, the Shipping Overview text section introduces policies, followed by the more detailed Shipping Methods data list, and finally the Returns Policy section. Error and loading states sit on top of or within this same stack, temporarily replacing or overlaying the normal content while maintaining the overall column structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The overall site width is a fixed 960–1000px. The left sidebar occupies roughly 25–30% of this width, with the right main content column taking the remaining 70–75%. Inside the main content column, a modest 10–16px padding is used on all sides. Vertically: the section navigation and overview together occupy roughly the top 30–35% of the main content column; the shipping methods list uses another ~35–40% as a dense table-like block; the returns policy fills the remaining ~25–30%, visually anchored toward the lower-right of the main content area. The loading indicator and error handler occupy a compact band or overlay in the central region of the main content column without altering the global header/sidebar proportions.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout preserves the high-density, utilitarian feel of the mockup: small to medium-small font sizes, tight line spacing, and compact vertical gaps between headings, paragraphs, and lists. The section navigation tabs are closely spaced, with minimal padding. Text sections use short 8–12px vertical spacing between lines of content and 12–16px between subsections. Tables and lists are tightly packed with narrow row heights. White space is modest everywhere except around the bottom portions of the main content column, where the last section has a bit more breathing room to echo the mockup’s relatively open product-image area.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a Z-like path analogous to the mockup: from the top-left logo (outside these components) to the bold global top navigation bar, then down the left sidebar categories, then across into the main content column. Within this main column, the eye lands first on the section navigation (sub-tabs) at the top, then moves slightly down-left to read the Shipping Overview copy. From there, it progresses downward into the dense Shipping Methods list, and finally settles on the Returns Policy section, which is visually weighted and aligned slightly toward the bottom-right of the main content area. Error and loading states are centered in the main column, interrupting this flow only when necessary but still respecting the top-to-bottom structure.

layout.overall_description: Below the global logo and red horizontal navigation bar, the page splits into the familiar two-column structure: a tall, purple-toned left sidebar filled with tightly stacked category links, and a wider right column for content. The Shipping & Returns experience unfolds entirely in that right column. At the very top of this column, just under the site’s main nav, a skeuomorphic, glossy sub-navigation bar for the Shipping & Returns sections spans most of the content width, echoing the early-2000s tab style. Beneath this sits a compact but text-heavy Shipping Overview section aligned to the left, providing a summary paragraph and a small bullet list. As you scroll down, the content densifies into a structured Shipping Methods list that reads like an old-school catalog table, occupying most of the mid-page space. The page concludes with a Returns Policy block anchored toward the lower-right of the column, with slightly more white space around it to mimic the original mockup’s emphasis on the lower-right area. When data is loading, a centered loading indicator floats over a lightly dimmed main content column; if an error occurs, a bold, colored error strip appears directly under the sub-navigation, pushing the normal sections downward. Overall, the page feels compact, utilitarian, and slightly dated, with a clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom scan pattern guided by strong horizontal bars and blocks of dense text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller occupies the entire right main content column from just below the global top navigation bar down to the footer area. It aligns flush with the right edge of the left sidebar and extends to the fixed site width’s right boundary, with 10–16px internal padding on all sides. All other Shipping & Returns components (section navigation, overview, methods list, returns policy, loading, and error states) are rendered inside this controller, stacked vertically. Visually, the controller itself is mostly invisible, acting like a background layer that defines the available content area and establishes the coordinate system for centered overlays (loading indicator) and inline system messages (error handler).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static, relatively positioned container for all Shipping & Returns content. It does not scroll independently; instead, it scrolls with the page as part of the main content column. It provides relative positioning for absolutely positioned overlays such as the loading indicator, ensuring that these overlays are centered within the main content column rather than the entire viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation is positioned at the very top of the Shipping & Returns main content column, inside the page controller, directly beneath the global header/navigation. It sits about 8–12px below the bottom edge of the top nav bar, spanning roughly 80–90% of the width of the main content column and aligned flush to the left, leaving a narrow margin on the right. Visually, it appears as a horizontal strip of small, glossy tabs (e.g., “Overview”, “Shipping Methods”, “Returns & Exchanges”) with each tab tightly spaced and separated by narrow gaps or subtle separators. The height of this bar is modest (about 40–50px), with minimal vertical padding around the tab labels to maintain a dense feel. There is only a small 8–10px gap between this navigation and the content sections immediately below, so the sub-tab bar feels visually connected to the text that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal bar anchored to the top of the main content column within the page controller. It scrolls away with the content rather than remaining sticky, reflecting the early-2000s non-sticky behavior. When an error state appears, the error message is inserted just below this navigation bar, so the tab structure remains the uppermost element inside the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Shipping Overview section appears immediately below the section navigation bar, separated by a tight 8–10px vertical gap. It aligns to the left edge of the main content column and spans about 90% of the column’s width, leaving a small consistent margin on the right. Vertically, this section occupies roughly the upper 25–30% of the main content area allocated to this page (above the fold on typical screens), with a compact headline (e.g., “Shipping Overview”) followed by one or two dense paragraphs and a short bullet list of key points. Line spacing is tight, and vertical gaps between heading, body text, and bullets are minimal (8–12px), giving the section a catalog-like density. The right side of the section feels slightly lighter, with the main text block favoring the left two-thirds of the area, subtly encouraging the eye to move downward and right toward the content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section that participates in the natural vertical document flow within the page controller. It expands vertically based on content but is expected to remain relatively compact. It pushes the shipping methods list downward and is itself pushed down if an error bar is inserted above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Shipping Methods list sits directly beneath the Shipping Overview section, with a narrow 10–12px vertical gap between them. It spans the full usable width of the main content column inside the controller (approximately 94–96% of the column, accounting for internal padding), giving it the presence of a full-width table or catalog grid. The list is presented in a dense, row-based format—similar to a compact shipping rate table—with columns for method name, estimated delivery time, and notes. Row heights are relatively short, and vertical spacing between rows is tight, reinforcing the utilitarian catalog feel. This block takes up a substantial portion of the middle of the page, roughly 35–40% of the main content column’s height, forming a visually solid horizontal band. The lower right portion of this list extends slightly farther down than the left (due to staggered row content), subtly continuing the top-left to bottom-right visual bias.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding data block within the main content column. It scrolls along with the rest of the page. Its height grows with the number of shipping methods but it always remains anchored directly under the overview section and directly above the returns policy section. No fixed header or sticky behavior is applied to this list in keeping with the early-2000s style.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Returns Policy section is placed immediately below the Shipping Methods list, with a compact 12–16px vertical gap. To echo the original mockup’s emphasis on the lower-right area, this section is slightly inset from the left: it starts about 10–15% in from the left edge of the main content column while still extending all the way to the right padding, creating a block that visually hugs the lower-right corner of the content area. It occupies roughly the bottom 25–30% of the main content column’s vertical space. The content is formatted as a dense, multi-subheader text block—short headings (e.g., “Return Window”, “Condition of Items”, “Exchange Process”) stacked closely with paragraphs or brief bullet lists underneath each. The overall spacing is tight, but the section may be enclosed in a faint bordered panel or slightly tinted background to give it a discrete, anchored feel at the bottom of the page, akin to a prominent callout area where a product photo might otherwise be in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that closes out the content inside the page controller. It scrolls naturally and expands vertically if the policy text is long, but its default design assumes a compact, bottom-right-anchored panel. It does not overlap other components; instead, it is pushed downward as the shipping methods list grows or pulled upward if that list is short.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error handler appears as a horizontal, banner-like block within the main content column, directly beneath the section navigation bar and above all other sections. It spans nearly the full width of the main content column (about 95–100% of the internal width) and has a slightly taller height than a normal text line (roughly 50–60px), making it immediately noticeable. The bar uses a strong background color (e.g., desaturated red or amber) in keeping with early-2000s alert styles, with an error icon on the left and tightly packed lines of explanatory text and a small ‘Retry’ button on the right. The top and bottom padding is modest (8–10px), keeping the bar compact and dense. When visible, it pushes the Shipping Overview section and all subsequent content downward; the rest of the layout remains intact beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, static block that appears within the normal vertical document flow inside the page controller. It is inserted immediately below the section navigation component. It does not overlay content; instead, it reflows the layout by occupying vertical space and pushing other sections down. Once the error is resolved or dismissed, this block is removed and the space collapses, allowing the overview section to move back up beneath the section navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator is centered within the visible area of the main content column, appearing on top of a subtly dimmed version of the content area when data is being fetched. It consists of a small spinner or progress animation and a single line of tightly set explanatory text (e.g., “Loading shipping and returns information…”), all contained within a compact box roughly 250–300px wide and 80–100px tall. This box is visually distinct but not overly large, maintaining the catalog-like density by avoiding excessive white space. It is horizontally centered between the left edge of the main content column and the right site boundary, and vertically centered between the bottom of the section navigation bar and the top of the viewport (or the mid-height of the controller), so it appears in the user’s immediate focus area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned overlay within the page controller, aligned using the controller as a relative positioning context. While active, it sits above other content sections (overview, list, returns) and may be accompanied by a semi-transparent backdrop that covers the rest of the main content column, preventing interaction. It scrolls with the page in the sense that it remains centered within the controller’s viewport, but it is typically shown only during brief loading phases before content is fully rendered.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays the dynamically loaded privacy policy, including data practices for browsing and checkout, with in-page navigation and deep-linking to specific sections.

core_features: Fetch and display the full privacy policy content from the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface, including last-updated metadata and all sections.
Generate a dynamic table of contents (TOC) from the returned sections to allow quick navigation within the privacy policy.
Support deep-linking to a specific privacy policy section using a URL query parameter (e.g., privacy_policy.html?section=<sectionId>).
Automatically scroll to and highlight the section specified in the URL parameter when the page loads or when the URL changes via browser navigation.
Allow users to expand or collapse individual sections (if desired) while keeping the current section reflected in the URL state.
Handle loading and error states for the privacy policy fetch operation, ensuring the user understands when content is loading or unavailable.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Accessing the privacy policy from the footer
1. User clicks the 'Privacy Policy' link in the global footer (navigates to privacy_policy.html without additional parameters).
2. The page controller initializes and calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. While awaiting the response, a loading state is shown.
4. Once the response arrives, the page displays the last-updated information and the list of sections.
5. The TOC is generated from the section titles; the first section is optionally brought into view if no URL parameter is specified.
6. User scrolls or clicks items in the TOC to navigate between sections.

Workflow: Opening a specific section via deep link
1. User follows a link such as privacy_policy.html?section=<sectionId> from another page or external source.
2. On page load, the controller parses the section query parameter and stores it as the target section.
3. The page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent and shows a loading state.
4. After the content loads, the controller locates the section whose identifier matches the section query parameter (for example, by matching an index or a generated ID based on the section’s position).
5. The page scrolls to the matched section and visually highlights it as active; if sections are collapsible, that section is expanded.
6. User can modify the current section by clicking other TOC items; each click updates the section query parameter, enabling a sharable URL for that section.

Workflow: Navigating sections using the table of contents
1. User views the privacy policy with all sections rendered and a TOC visible.
2. User clicks a TOC entry corresponding to a particular section.
3. The TOC click handler prevents full page reload, updates the URL query parameter section=<targetSectionId> using the History API, and scrolls to the corresponding section anchor.
4. The clicked TOC entry is marked as active, and the associated section is highlighted; if applicable, that section is expanded while others may remain as they are or be collapsed based on design.
5. User can use the browser Back/Forward buttons to navigate between previously viewed sections; on each popstate event, the deep-link handler reads the new section query parameter and updates the active section accordingly.

Workflow: Handling loading and error conditions
1. User navigates to privacy_policy.html.
2. The controller calls getPrivacyPolicyContent and sets state to loading.
3. If the request succeeds, loading state is cleared, and the policy content is rendered.
4. If the request fails (e.g., network error or server error), the controller sets an error state.
5. The page displays an error message along with an option to retry loading (e.g., a 'Retry' button).
6. On retry, the controller calls getPrivacyPolicyContent again and repeats the loading flow.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: Browser navigates to privacy_policy.html (with or without section query parameter).
- Behavior: The page controller parses URL parameters, initializes loading state, and calls getPrivacyPolicyContent. Once data is received, it renders the header, last-updated information, TOC, and sections. If a section parameter exists, it scrolls to and highlights that section.

Interaction: TOC item click
- Trigger: User clicks a TOC entry representing a specific section.
- Behavior: Prevents default full-page navigation, updates the URL query parameter section=<targetSectionId> via the History API, sets the active section in internal state, and scrolls to the corresponding section element. The clicked entry is visually marked as active.

Interaction: Section header click (expand/collapse)
- Trigger: User clicks a section header (if collapsible behavior is implemented).
- Behavior: Toggles an expanded/collapsed state for that section. Optionally, when a section is expanded, the controller updates the section query parameter to reflect the currently focused section. The body_html for that section is shown or hidden accordingly.

Interaction: Browser Back/Forward navigation
- Trigger: User clicks the browser Back or Forward buttons after navigating between sections via TOC clicks.
- Behavior: The deep-link handler listens to popstate events, reads the current section query parameter, updates the active section state, and scrolls to the corresponding section without refetching the policy content.

Interaction: Retry loading on error
- Trigger: User clicks a 'Retry' action when an error state is shown.
- Behavior: Clears any previous error state, sets loading state to true, and calls getPrivacyPolicyContent again. On success, renders the content; on failure, shows the error state again.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL query parameters to manage navigational state and deep-linking, without using localStorage.

Query Parameters:
- section: Represents the currently focused section of the privacy policy. The value is a section identifier that the page determines from the getPrivacyPolicyContent response (for example, by using the section's index or a derived ID). Examples: privacy_policy.html?section=0 or privacy_policy.html?section=intro.

State Behavior:
1. Initial Load:
   - On page load, the controller reads window.location.search and extracts the section parameter if present.
   - It stores this value in internal state as targetSectionId.
   - It then calls getPrivacyPolicyContent. Once the sections are available, it resolves targetSectionId to a specific section (e.g., by matching index or generated ID) and scrolls to that section.

2. Updating URL on Interaction:
   - When the user clicks a TOC entry or expands a specific section (if used as the main focus), the controller updates the section parameter in the URL using history.pushState or history.replaceState, without reloading the page.
   - Internal state activeSectionId is updated to match the new section parameter.

3. Reacting to URL Changes:
   - The page listens to popstate events so that when the user uses the browser Back/Forward buttons, the section parameter is re-read.
   - On each change, the controller updates activeSectionId and scrolls to the corresponding section, ensuring the view matches the URL state.

4. No Parameter Case:
   - If no section parameter is provided, the page loads all sections and may either leave all sections fully visible or set a default activeSectionId (e.g., the first section) without adding a parameter to the URL. Any subsequent user navigation can then introduce the section parameter.

5. No use of localStorage:
   - All navigation and state restoration across page loads relies solely on the section query parameter and standard browser history behavior, ensuring predictable deep-linking and bookmarkable URLs.

components.functionality: Coordinates data fetching from getPrivacyPolicyContent, parses URL parameters, manages loading/error/active-section state, and passes structured data and callbacks to child components.
Displays the static page title and the dynamically loaded last-updated information from the privacy policy data.
Renders a dynamic table of contents based on the privacy policy sections, allowing users to click to navigate directly to a section and updating the active section state and URL query parameter.

Renders all privacy policy sections returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent, each with a unique anchor ID, and optionally supports expand/collapse behavior per section while highlighting the currently active section.
Synchronizes the section query parameter in the URL with the active section state and scroll position, both on initial load and when the URL changes via browser navigation.
Displays a loading indicator while the privacy policy content is being fetched and hides it when loading is complete or an error occurs.
Displays an error message and retry action when getPrivacyPolicyContent fails, allowing the user to trigger another fetch attempt.
