layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width two-column layout, likely based on an old-school table or float structure rather than a formal modern grid (approx. 960–1000px total width, left column ~25%, right column ~75%).
layout.layout_pattern: The privacy policy page sits inside the existing early-2000s catalog shell (global logo and top nav above, global category/sidebar nav to the far left). Within the main right content area, the privacy policy introduces its own mini version of the classic layout: a full-width page header at the top of the main column, and beneath it an internal two-column structure with a strong left in-page navigation (the privacy policy TOC) and the detailed policy sections on the right. Content flows top-to-bottom: page title, brief introductory copy, then an in-page navigation list, then vertically stacked policy sections. This preserves the mockup’s emphasis on left-hand navigation feeding into dense right-hand content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The privacy policy page sits inside the existing early-2000s catalog shell (global logo and top nav above, global category/sidebar nav to the far left). Within the main right content area, the privacy policy introduces its own mini version of the classic layout: a full-width page header at the top of the main column, and beneath it an internal two-column structure with a strong left in-page navigation (the privacy policy TOC) and the detailed policy sections on the right. Content flows top-to-bottom: page title, brief introductory copy, then an in-page navigation list, then vertically stacked policy sections. This preserves the mockup’s emphasis on left-hand navigation feeding into dense right-hand content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The overall page retains a fixed-width feel of roughly 960–1000px centered on the viewport. The global site shell consumes the full width, with the main content column (where these components live) occupying roughly 70–75% of the page width on the right, while the global category sidebar sits to the far left at around 25–30%. Inside the main content column, the privacy-policy-header spans its full width at the top, occupying about 15–20% of the main content height above the fold. Below the header, the internal left TOC column takes about 25–30% of the main content width, and the right policy text column takes the remaining 70–75%. Vertically, the TOC runs down most of the available height, while the right content column grows with the length of the policy; loading and error states momentarily occupy the right column’s space where the sections would otherwise appear.
layout_strategies.content_density: Typography and spacing are compact: relatively small font sizes with tight line-height, modest 10–14px interior padding, and minimal gaps between headings, paragraphs, and list items. The TOC links are stacked closely with small vertical spacing, echoing the dense category navigation from the mockup. Section headings in the right column are slightly larger and bold, but still not spacious. The only relatively open feeling is in the right content column margins and around any horizontal rules or section separators, which create subtle breathing room without becoming modern-minimal. Loading and error messages are contained in fairly tight, rectangular bands with only modest padding, consistent with a utilitarian, information-dense catalog aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual path starts at the global logo and top navigation, then moves down into the main content where the privacy-policy-header acts as the first focal point. From there, the eye is pulled to the stronger color block of the left in-page TOC column, which continues the mockup’s strong left-navigation motif. Users then scan right into the main policy text column to read details. As they scroll, the TOC remains visually anchored (via sticky behavior) along the left edge of the main content, continually drawing the eye back to left-hand navigation before re-entering the right-hand body text. This creates a repeated Z-like motion within the main content area: header, left TOC, right sections, then downward through the text, echoing the diagonal top-left to bottom-right emphasis of the original product layout but applied to legal copy instead of product imagery.

layout.overall_description: Within the fixed-width catalog page, the global logo and glossy top navigation bar sit across the top, with the familiar purple-toned category sidebar hugging the far left. To the right of that sidebar, the privacy policy content occupies a white main panel. At the very top of this panel, a horizontal header block runs full-width, styled like an old-school content tab: a light gradient or flat pale bar with a bold “Privacy Policy” title aligned to the left and a short, single-line description just beneath or beside it, all tightly padded. Directly below, the panel splits into two inner columns. On the left, a narrow colored TOC band (around one quarter of the panel width) runs from just under the header downward, filled with compact, stacked links for each major policy section—visually echoing the site’s main left navigation but scoped to this page. On the right, a wider white column presents the actual policy content as a series of closely spaced section headings and paragraphs, stretching downward and defining the main reading area. When loading or error states appear, they occupy the right column where the sections would show, appearing as tight rectangular information blocks while the header and overall structure remain intact. The overall effect is a dense, utilitarian, early-2000s corporate layout: strong top bar, strong left navigation, and a text-heavy right-hand content area that guides the user from the top header to the left TOC and then into the main body of the policy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller acts as the structural container for all privacy policy content within the main right-hand column of the site. It is positioned directly beneath the global red horizontal navigation bar and immediately to the right of the site’s global left sidebar, inheriting the main content area’s fixed width (approximately 700–750px, corresponding to about 70–75% of the total 960–1000px page width). Vertically, it runs from just below the global header down to the bottom of the page content, expanding with the length of the policy. Inside this container, the top 15–20% of its visible height (near the fold) is reserved for the privacy-policy-header spanning the full internal width. Below that, the controller defines an internal two-column structure: a left column for the privacy-policy-toc at roughly 25–30% of the controller’s width, and a right column for the privacy-policy-section-list (or loading/error states) taking the remaining 70–75%. Both internal columns start aligned under the header’s bottom edge and extend downward; the left column can be shorter or equal in height to the right column depending on how many TOC items exist. The controller itself does not render any visible styling beyond aligning and constraining its children within the main content panel; it simply preserves the fixed-width, table-like look of the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container in the main content area that establishes the internal two-column layout for its children; it scrolls with the page, with no fixed or sticky positioning itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy header sits at the very top of the page controller, flush with its left and right edges, forming a horizontal band that visually mimics an early-2000s content tab. It spans 100% of the controller’s width (roughly 700–750px) and has a modest height of about 80–120px depending on the amount of introductory text. The header’s background is a pale grey or gently gradient strip that subtly stands apart from the pure white body area below, with a 1px bottom border or shadow to create a clear separation. The main title, “Privacy Policy,” is aligned to the left with only 10–16px of padding from the left edge, using a slightly larger, bold font but still compact line height. Directly below the title (or to its right if the copy is short), a single short descriptive sentence explains the page’s purpose, aligned left and using a smaller, regular-weight font. Vertical spacing between the title and description is tight (around 4–8px), in keeping with the dense feel. The bottom edge of this header band acts as the alignment baseline for the columns below: the top of the privacy-policy-toc and the top of the privacy-policy-section-list (or loading/error content) sit just beneath it, with only a narrow gap of 6–10px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width band at the top of the page controller; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick, serving as the anchor point for the internal columns below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy table of contents forms the internal left navigation column within the page controller, reinforcing the strong left-nav motif from the mockup. It starts immediately beneath the privacy-policy-header, aligned with the controller’s left edge, and extends downward alongside the main content column. Its width is approximately 25–30% of the page controller’s width (roughly 180–220px in a 700–750px main content area), giving it a tall, narrow, sidebar-like profile. The background is a solid or slightly gradient purple or blue-purple block, visually similar to the site’s global category sidebar but slightly lighter to differentiate it as in-page navigation. Inside, TOC items are stacked vertically from top to bottom with minimal spacing—around 4–6px of vertical gap between links and 8–12px of padding from the left edge—creating a dense list. Each link’s text is left-aligned, set in a small, high-contrast font (e.g., white or off-white) with tight line-height to allow multiple items to be visible without scrolling. The very top of the TOC may include a small, bold label such as “On this page:” aligned left, separated by only a thin horizontal rule or a slightly stronger font weight rather than extra whitespace. The TOC’s vertical span is at least as tall as the initial viewport area under the header and may continue beyond the fold if there are many entries; any excess content scrolls within the page, not within the TOC itself. On larger viewports, the TOC visually tracks alongside the policy text, forming a continuous left-colored edge for the main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a static left column within the page controller but with sticky behavior relative to the controller’s top: once scrolled into view, it remains stuck to the top of the viewport (just below the global header) as the right-hand policy content scrolls, maintaining a persistent left-nav feel without leaving its column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy section list occupies the main right-hand content column inside the page controller. It is positioned immediately to the right of the privacy-policy-toc, with a narrow horizontal gap of about 10–16px between them, and it starts just below the bottom edge of the privacy-policy-header (aligned vertically with the top of the TOC). This column uses the remaining 70–75% of the page controller’s width (roughly 480–560px). The background is white, matching the overall main content panel, with modest internal padding of 12–16px on all sides, giving the policy text a slightly more open feel than the dense TOC but still compact overall. Inside this column, individual policy sections are stacked vertically from top to bottom. Each section begins with a bold, slightly larger heading, left-aligned, with only 6–10px of space above and 4–8px below. Paragraphs within each section use small, tightly spaced text, with around 4–6px between paragraphs and 10–12px between sections. Where lists are used (e.g., for types of data collected), bullet points are indented modestly and stacked closely, reflecting the catalog’s dense information style rather than modern airy lists. Each section is anchored with a slight invisible buffer (e.g., 40–60px of offset space above) so that when navigated to via deep link, the heading appears comfortably below any sticky elements, preserving legibility. The bottom of this column extends well below the initial viewport as the policy content grows, forming a continuous reading strip from top to bottom, with the colored TOC column flanking its left edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content column that grows vertically with the number of policy sections; it scrolls independently of the sticky TOC (though within the same page), and when loading or error states are active, it is temporarily hidden or replaced in this same space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The deep link handler is a non-visual component that resides logically at the top of the page controller, coordinating the relationship between the URL (hash or query parameters) and the positions of the sections in the privacy-policy-section-list and entries in the privacy-policy-toc. It has no visible width, height, or styling and does not change the dimensions or alignment of surrounding content. Conceptually, it is layered above the structural layout: when a user clicks a TOC item or lands on a deep-linked URL, it calculates the correct scroll position for the corresponding section heading within the right content column, ensuring that the heading appears just below any sticky global or TOC elements. It also updates which TOC item is visually marked as active (e.g., via bold weight or a small colored indicator bar), but the visual representation of that active state remains confined to the existing footprint of the TOC.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering, purely logical component with no physical footprint; it operates in the background, synchronizing scroll positions and URL state without affecting spatial layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading state occupies the same general area that the privacy-policy-section-list would use, while leaving the overall layout frame (header plus two-column structure) intact. When active, it appears directly beneath the privacy-policy-header, aligned with the right-hand content column’s left edge (i.e., starting just to the right of the TOC and using the same width of approximately 70–75% of the page controller). Its height is modest—typically around 80–140px—forming a compact rectangular panel. The background remains white to blend with the content column, but the loading state is delineated by a light grey border or subtle top-and-bottom lines. Inside, a small spinner or progress indicator is placed toward the top-left of this panel, aligned roughly with the first line of text in normal content, with only 10–16px of padding. To the right of the spinner (on the same line), a short status message like “Loading privacy policy…” appears in a standard small font. There is minimal extra whitespace above or below; the block ends soon after the message, perhaps with a secondary, even smaller line of status text directly beneath if needed. Visually, the dense TOC remains visible and unchanged on the left, reinforcing the fixed structure while the right side temporarily shows the loading block instead of the full policy text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the privacy-policy-section-list in the right content column while data is being fetched; it scrolls with the page and maintains its position under the header and to the right of the TOC, disappearing once the content has loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state also occupies the right-hand content column where the policy text would normally appear, using the same width and horizontal alignment as the privacy-policy-section-list (starting just to the right of the TOC and spanning about 70–75% of the page controller’s width). It sits immediately under the privacy-policy-header, replacing any loading or content panels when an error occurs. The error block is slightly more visually pronounced than the loading state: a pale red or pink-tinted background spans a compact rectangular area roughly 100–160px tall, with a thin, darker red border at the top or around the entire block. Inside, an error icon (such as a small exclamation symbol within a triangle), sized modestly, appears near the top-left corner with 10–16px padding from the edge. To its right, on the same line, the primary error message (“We’re unable to load the privacy policy right now.”) is set in a standard small font, bolded or colored slightly darker for emphasis. Below this, a secondary line of smaller, regular-weight text provides brief guidance (e.g., “Please refresh the page or try again later.”), with minimal vertical spacing—only about 4–8px. If a retry button is present, it sits immediately below the text, left-aligned, with only a modest top margin of about 6–10px. The block does not extend to fill unused vertical space; it remains a tight panel at the top of the content area, leaving the rest of the white right column empty but visually framed by the persistent left TOC and the header above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the right content column’s normal content (and any loading indicator) when an error occurs; it scrolls with the page, maintaining its fixed position beneath the header and to the right of the TOC until dismissed or the page is reloaded.

name: Help & FAQ
title: Help & FAQ
description: Help center page where users can browse, filter, and search frequently asked questions about tarps and covers, and navigate to related policy pages.

core_features: Fetch and display all FAQs using the getFaqList interface.
Group and filter FAQs by their category field from getFaqList (e.g., materials, sizing, ordering, shipping_returns, privacy), with category options derived dynamically from the returned data.
Provide in-page search over the loaded FAQs (question and answer_html content) without additional API calls.
Support deep-linking and URL-driven state so users can open the page with a specific category, search query, or FAQ item preselected.
Render FAQ answers from answer_html, allowing inline links within answers to navigate to shipping_returns.html and privacy_policy.html.
Provide basic troubleshooting guidance discoverable via category and search (content entirely supplied by getFaqList).
user_workflows:
Browse all FAQs:
1) User opens help_faq.html without URL parameters.
2) Page calls getFaqList and waits for the full FAQ list.
3) Once loaded, the page derives the available categories from the category field of each FAQ.
4) All FAQs are shown in a list (optionally grouped by category); none or only URL-indicated items are expanded initially.
5) User scrolls and clicks individual FAQ questions to expand or collapse their answers.

Filter FAQs by category:
1) User opens help_faq.html or is already on the page.
2) Category filter component reads existing URL parameter (if any) and highlights the active category, or defaults to an 'all categories' view if no parameter is present.
3) User selects a category from the dynamically generated list of categories.
4) The URL is updated to include category=<selectedCategory> (preserving any existing query or faqId if appropriate).
5) The FAQ list immediately filters to show only FAQs whose category matches the selectedCategory value.

Search within FAQs:
1) User types a search phrase into the FAQ search input.
2) User submits the form (or triggers search via a dedicated control).
3) The URL is updated with q=<encodedSearchQuery> (preserving any existing category or faqId parameters).
4) The page filters the already loaded FAQ list client-side by matching the query against each FAQ's question and (text-extracted) answer_html.
5) Only FAQs matching the query (and any active category filter) remain visible; user can refine or clear the query, which updates the URL and the results.

Open a specific FAQ via deep link:
1) User follows a link to help_faq.html with a faqId URL parameter (e.g., help_faq.html?faqId=<someId> plus optional category or q).
2) Page calls getFaqList and waits for the full FAQ list.
3) After data is loaded, the page searches for the FAQ with id matching faqId.
4) If found, the page ensures this FAQ is visible by adjusting filters if necessary and automatically expanding its answer.
5) The view optionally scrolls to the expanded FAQ so it is in viewport.

Navigate from FAQ answers to policy pages:
1) User expands an FAQ whose answer_html includes inline links to related policies.
2) User clicks a link within answer_html that points to shipping_returns.html.
3) Browser navigates to shipping_returns.html (no additional script handling required beyond normal link navigation).
4) Similarly, if user clicks an inline link pointing to privacy_policy.html, browser navigates to privacy_policy.html.

Refine or reset FAQ view via URL parameters:
1) User interacts with category filters, search, and FAQ expansion.
2) Each interaction updates the URL parameters (category, q, faqId, and optionally others) via pushState/replaceState.
3) User can bookmark or share the current URL, preserving the state of filters and the focused FAQ.
4) When the link is revisited, the page re-applies the stored state after calling getFaqList, recreating the same view without any additional user input.
interactions: Category selection: Clicking a category option updates the active category filter, filters the FAQ list, and updates the URL parameter category.
Search submission: Submitting the search form sets or updates the q parameter, filters the FAQs client-side, and refreshes the visible list without additional API calls.

FAQ expansion: Clicking on an FAQ question toggles its expanded/collapsed state; when expanded, the faqId parameter is updated to that FAQ's id (and may be cleared or changed when collapsed or when another FAQ is selected).

URL-driven initialization: On initial page load or when the URL changes (e.g., via browser back/forward navigation), the page reinterprets category, q, and faqId parameters and re-applies filters and expansion to the already loaded FAQ data.

Inline policy link navigation: Clicking any <a> element inside answer_html that targets shipping_returns.html or privacy_policy.html navigates directly to those pages according to standard browser behavior.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is managed entirely via URL parameters and in-memory client state derived from getFaqList. No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used.

URL Parameters:
- category: Represents the currently selected FAQ category. When present, the FAQ list is filtered to items where faq.category === category. The list of available categories is derived dynamically from the category values present in the getFaqList response. If category is omitted or does not match any existing category, the page shows FAQs from all categories.

- q: Represents the current search query. When present and non-empty, the client filters the loaded FAQ array by checking whether the query string (case-insensitive) matches within the question text or a text-only version of answer_html. Filtering by q is applied in combination with category filtering if category is also set.

- faqId: Represents a specific FAQ the user wants to focus on. After getFaqList returns and filters are applied based on category and q, the page looks for an item with id === faqId:
  - If the item exists and passes the active filters, it is expanded and optionally scrolled into view.
  - If the item exists but does not pass the current filters (for example, it's in a different category than the one in the URL), the page can either: (a) override category to match the item's category, or (b) temporarily include the item regardless of category. The chosen behavior should be consistent, and the URL may be updated to reflect any overridden category.
  - If no matching item is found, faqId is ignored for display purposes, and the view behaves as though faqId is not set.

- Additional internal state flags (such as whether multiple FAQs can be expanded simultaneously or whether all results should be collapsed by default) are not represented in the URL, but expansion of the focused FAQ driven by faqId is always reflected in the URL.

State Transitions:
- On initial load:
  1) Parse URL parameters (category, q, faqId).
  2) Call getFaqList once and store the resulting array in memory.
  3) Derive the set of available categories from the category field of each FAQ.
  4) Apply category and q filters to generate a filteredFaqs collection.
  5) If faqId is present, locate the corresponding FAQ in the full list, ensure it is visible under the current filters (adjusting filters or URL if needed), and mark it as expanded.

- On user interactions:
  - Changing category: Updates category in the URL (and removes it if user chooses an all-categories option), recomputes filteredFaqs, and clears faqId unless the currently focused FAQ remains visible.
  - Updating search query: Updates q in the URL (removing q when cleared), recomputes filteredFaqs, and clears faqId unless the focused FAQ remains visible.
  - Clicking a FAQ question: Toggles that FAQ's expanded state and sets faqId to that id when expanded; when collapsed, faqId can be removed or set to another expanded FAQ if multiple expansions are allowed.

- On browser navigation (back/forward):
  - The page listens for URL changes and re-applies the above logic without re-calling getFaqList, using the already cached FAQ array.

components.functionality: Initializes the Help & FAQ page, calls getFaqList once on load, parses URL parameters, derives available categories, applies filters, manages the central FAQ state (full list, filtered list, active category, search query, focused FAQ), and coordinates updates between components.

Displays a selectable list of FAQ categories and an option to view all categories. The list of categories is generated dynamically from the category values in the FAQ data returned by getFaqList. When a category is selected, it informs the controller to update the active category filter and URL.

Provides an input field and submit control to search within FAQs. It reads the initial value from the q URL parameter and, upon submission or change (depending on implementation), notifies the controller to update the query, filter the list, and update the URL.

Renders the list of FAQs after filters are applied. It receives the filteredFaqs collection and the currently focusedFaqId from the controller, and for each FAQ it renders an interactive item that can be expanded or collapsed. It ensures that the FAQ whose id matches focusedFaqId is rendered in the expanded state.

Represents a single FAQ entry with a clickable question and a collapsible answer section. It displays the question text and renders the answer_html as HTML when expanded. It reports toggle actions back to the controller so that focusedFaqId and URL (faqId parameter) can be updated. It does not alter the inline links in answer_html; those links navigate normally.

Encapsulates reading and writing of URL parameters for the page. It parses category, q, and faqId from the URL on load and on browser navigation events, and exposes methods for other components (mainly the controller) to update these parameters using pushState/replaceState without reloading the page.

Displays loading, empty, or error states based on the lifecycle of the getFaqList call and the size of the resulting FAQ collection. It shows a loading indicator while data is being fetched, an informational message if no FAQs are returned, and an error message if getFaqList fails.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page sits inside the existing site chrome: logo and global horizontal navigation span the full width at the top. Directly beneath the nav, the content area is a fixed-width, center-aligned container (~960–1000px). This container splits into two columns: a left sidebar for FAQ categories and related help navigation, and a wider right column for search, FAQ results, and answers. Within the right column, content flows vertically: page title (implicitly part of the main area), then the FAQ search form, then the FAQ list. Individual FAQ items are stacked top-to-bottom inside the list, with answers expanding downward. layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header (logo + red horizontal nav) consumes the top ~15–20% of the viewport height. Below it, the content row takes the remaining height. The left sidebar (faq-category-filter) occupies about 25–30% of the content width from the left edge of the fixed container, running almost the full height of the page beneath the header. layout_strategies.content_density: All interactive and textual elements are tightly packed. Category links in the left sidebar are closely stacked with minimal line height and small vertical gaps. The search form is compact, with the input, dropdowns (if any), and button sitting on a single line or two lines with tight spacing. FAQ items are listed with small font sizes, tight leading, and thin dividers rather than large blocks of whitespace. Padding inside boxes and panels is functional rather than generous, echoing an early-2000s utilitarian catalog look. layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the logo in the top-left, then is quickly pulled to the horizontal red navigation bar that stretches across the top. From this bar, the user’s attention drops into the left column, where the solid, slightly glossy sidebar panel (faq-category-filter) presents a vertical stack of help categories. After scanning categories, the user’s gaze shifts right into the main content, first hitting the Help & FAQ title and the search bar at the top of the right column. From there, the eye moves downward through the FAQ list and into individual FAQ items, following a Z-like path: top-left logo, across the top navigation, down the left sidebar navigation, then across to the search form and down through the list of questions and answers.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the full-width logo and red horizontal navigation bar, a fixed-width content container (around 960–1000px) is centered on the page. This container is split into a strong two-column layout: on the left, a tall purple-toned sidebar panel runs nearly from just below the navigation to near the footer, occupying about a quarter of the width and packed with stacked FAQ categories and related links. On the right, a wider main content panel takes up the remaining width with a white or very light background. At the top of this right panel, the Help & FAQ heading and a compact search form sit in a slightly glossy sub-header strip. Immediately below, the FAQ list fills the column with dense, stacked question rows separated by thin rules. Expanding a question reveals its answer text in-place, pushing the content below it down while staying within the same column. The overall look is structured and utilitarian, with modest padding, tight spacing, and a clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading path guided by strong color blocks in the header and sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ page controller exists as an invisible structural wrapper that spans the entire content region between the global header (logo + red horizontal navigation) and the site footer. It defines a fixed-width, center-aligned layout container approximately 960–1000px wide. Within this container, it establishes two primary vertical columns: a left sidebar column occupying about 25–30% of the width, and a right main content column occupying about 70–75%. Both columns start at the same horizontal line just below the red navigation bar and extend downward in parallel. The controller ensures that the left sidebar (faq-category-filter) and the main content (faq-search-form + faq-list) align in height and share consistent top and side padding (about 12–16px from the container edges). It also reserves the vertical ordering in the main column so that the search form always appears above the FAQ list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural container. Establishes a fixed-width, center-aligned two-column layout that scrolls with the page; not sticky or fixed, simply defining document flow for its child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category filter panel occupies the entire left column directly beneath the global navigation bar. It starts flush under the red nav, with a narrow gap of about 8–10px from the container’s top padding. Horizontally, it stretches from the left boundary of the fixed-width container to roughly 25–30% of the container’s width. The panel has a solid or gradient purple background with a slightly beveled, early-2000s-style header bar at its top reading something like “Help Topics” or “FAQ Categories.” Inside, categories are presented as a dense, vertical list of links with small font size, minimal line height, and only 4–6px vertical spacing between items. Sections may be grouped with subtle subheaders in bold or a slightly darker background strip. The panel runs downward nearly to the bottom of the page’s main content area, giving the impression of a full-height navigation rail that anchors the left side of the layout. Any internal scroll needed for many categories happens inside this column, but in most cases the panel simply scrolls with the page as one tall block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static left sidebar within the main content container. It scrolls with the page and maintains a fixed width (25–30% of container) relative to the main column; not sticky or floating, just a tall, anchored navigation column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ search form is positioned at the very top of the right main content column, immediately to the right of the category filter. It aligns horizontally with the top of the left sidebar, sitting just below the global navigation bar with a similar 8–10px top padding. The form spans roughly 90–95% of the right column’s width, leaving a small 10–16px right margin against the container edge and a similar gap on its left from the column divider. Visually, it sits within a shallow, light-colored panel or strip, potentially with a subtle gradient or beveled top edge to echo the skeuomorphic style. The main search input field occupies about 60–70% of this strip’s width, left-aligned, with a compact height and slightly rounded or beveled borders. Any category or type dropdowns sit immediately to the right of the input, with a small 4–8px gap between controls. A prominent but compact search button, aligned to the right of the input (and any dropdowns), completes the row. The form’s vertical height is modest—approximately 60–80px total including padding—giving it a compact, tool-like feel. Below the form, a small 6–10px gap separates it from the top of the FAQ list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the right column. It remains anchored above the FAQ list and moves up/down as the page scrolls; no sticky behavior. Width responsively tracks the right column’s width within the fixed page container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ list occupies the remaining vertical space of the right main column directly below the search form. It begins about 6–10px beneath the bottom edge of the search form’s panel, spanning roughly the same width (about 90–95% of the right column). The list is a vertical stack of FAQ items that fills the column from this starting point downwards, extending below the fold as needed. Each FAQ item is rendered as a full-width row within this list, with a tight top and bottom margin (4–8px) to maintain high density. Thin horizontal lines or subtle alternating background stripes may separate items, in keeping with early-2000s catalog tables. The list aligns its left edge with the search input above, creating a clean vertical line from search to questions. As answers expand, the list grows downward, pushing subsequent FAQ items and the page’s bottom boundary further down, but always constrained within the fixed-width right column. Any loading or empty states for search/filter results display within this same area, centralized horizontally but aligned to the left edge to preserve the dense layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable vertical list within the right content column. It participates in the normal document flow, expanding vertically as more FAQs or expanded answers are shown. No fixed height container; instead, the browser scroll bar handles overflow for the entire page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each FAQ item is a horizontal slice within the FAQ list, occupying nearly 100% of the list’s width, minus a small inner padding of about 8–12px on all sides. The question text appears on the first line in a slightly bolder weight and slightly larger size than the body copy, aligned to the left with no icon or a small, simple plus/minus icon on the far left or right. The question row height is compact, with only 4–6px vertical padding, resulting in a tight, dense appearance. When an item is collapsed, only the question row is visible. When expanded, the answer appears directly beneath the question within the same item block, indented slightly (e.g., an extra 8–12px left padding) and using small, tightly spaced text. Bullet lists or short paragraphs in the answer are stacked with minimal spacing, echoing the dense product spec lists from the catalog style. A thin horizontal separator line appears at the bottom of the item to distinguish it from the next one. The item expands downward only, pushing subsequent items down while maintaining alignment with the overall right column boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the FAQ list. It expands and collapses vertically within the normal flow of the list without overlapping other items. No floating or fixed positioning; each item’s height adjusts based on answer visibility.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is non-visual and occupies no explicit space on the page. Conceptually, it is layered behind the FAQ page controller, listening to interactions with the left sidebar category filter, the search form, and individual FAQ items. It updates the browser’s URL query parameters or hash as the user filters by category, searches, or opens a specific FAQ, but does so without rendering any elements. It aligns logically with the page’s main container and columns but has no width, height, or visual style, ensuring that it does not disrupt the fixed-width two-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered logic layer. It does not influence layout or occupy space; it simply hooks into the browser history and URL while the visual components behave according to the established two-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading state appears within the right main content column, occupying the same horizontal space as the FAQ list area. When active (for example, after changing a category or submitting a search), it is centered vertically within the currently visible portion of the FAQ list region, but aligned to the left margin of the list to maintain the utilitarian feel. It consists of a compact horizontal strip or small panel—roughly the height of 1–2 FAQ items—containing a subtle spinner or progress indicator on the left and short explanatory text (e.g., “Loading FAQs…”) to its right. The panel spans about 60–70% of the list’s width with modest padding (8–12px). Behind it, either the previous FAQ items remain visible with reduced emphasis (slightly dimmed) or the area is left blank white, but the loading panel never extends into the left sidebar or over the header. The visual style uses the same light background and thin border treatment as the FAQ list, possibly with a faint gradient, so it feels native to the early-2000s catalog aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline overlay within the FAQ list region. It is positioned relative to the FAQ list container, centered horizontally within that container’s width and scrolling with the page. It does not fix to the viewport; instead, it appears where the list content would normally be, temporarily replacing or covering list items until loading completes.
